title: Mixed martial arts

infobox.value: [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation")

[MMA cage](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_cage "Mixed martial arts cage") (specially [octagonal](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship#Cage "Ultimate Fighting Championship")), [ring](/wiki/Boxing_ring "Boxing ring")

(**MMA**)[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a full-contact [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") based on [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") and [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling"), incorporating techniques from various [combat sports](/wiki/Combat_sports "Combat sports") from around the world.

took place throughout [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and the countries of [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). At the same time, in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") there was a phenomenon called [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo"), which became known for unrestricted fights between various styles such as [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu"), [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [luta livre](/wiki/Luta_livre "Luta livre"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") and [capoeira](/wiki/Capoeira "Capoeira"). An early high-profile mixed bout was [Kimura vs Gracie](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura_vs._H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie") in 1951. In mid-20th-century [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), rooftop [street fighting](/wiki/Street_fighting "Street fighting") contests between different [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") styles gave rise to [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee")'s [hybrid martial arts](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") style, [Jeet Kune Do](/wiki/Jeet_Kune_Do "Jeet Kune Do"). Another precursor to modern MMA was the 1976 [Ali vs. Inoki](/wiki/Ali_vs._Inoki "Ali vs. Inoki") exhibition bout, fought between boxer [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") and wrestler [Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Antonio_Inoki "Antonio Inoki") in Japan, where it later inspired the foundation of [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") in 1985, [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase") in 1993, and the [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") in 1997.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Full-contact combat sport

For the fighting styles that combine arts, see [Hybrid martial arts](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts").

"MMA" redirects here. For other uses, see [MMA (disambiguation)](/wiki/MMA_(disambiguation) "MMA (disambiguation)").

Mixed martial arts

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Junior dos Santos](/wiki/Junior_dos_Santos "Junior dos Santos") vs. [Shane Carwin](/wiki/Shane_Carwin "Shane Carwin"), the main event of [UFC 131](/wiki/UFC_131 "UFC 131") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") in 2011 | |

| Characteristics | |
| Contact | Full contact |
| Type | [Combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") |
| Venue | [MMA cage](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_cage "Mixed martial arts cage") (specially [octagonal](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship#Cage "Ultimate Fighting Championship")), [ring](/wiki/Boxing_ring "Boxing ring") |

**Mixed martial arts** (**MMA**)[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a full-contact [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") based on [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") and [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling"), incorporating techniques from various [combat sports](/wiki/Combat_sports "Combat sports") from around the world.

In the early 20th century, various **inter-stylistic contests** took place throughout [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and the countries of [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). At the same time, in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") there was a phenomenon called [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo"), which became known for unrestricted fights between various styles such as [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu"), [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [luta livre](/wiki/Luta_livre "Luta livre"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") and [capoeira](/wiki/Capoeira "Capoeira"). An early high-profile mixed bout was [Kimura vs Gracie](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura_vs._H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie") in 1951. In mid-20th-century [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), rooftop [street fighting](/wiki/Street_fighting "Street fighting") contests between different [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") styles gave rise to [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee")'s [hybrid martial arts](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") style, [Jeet Kune Do](/wiki/Jeet_Kune_Do "Jeet Kune Do"). Another precursor to modern MMA was the 1976 [Ali vs. Inoki](/wiki/Ali_vs._Inoki "Ali vs. Inoki") exhibition bout, fought between boxer [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") and wrestler [Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Antonio_Inoki "Antonio Inoki") in Japan, where it later inspired the foundation of [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") in 1985, [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase") in 1993, and the [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") in 1997.

In the 1990s, the [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family") brought their Brazilian jiu-jitsu style, first developed in Brazil from the 1920s, to the United States—which culminated in the founding of the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") (UFC) promotion company in 1993. The company held an event with almost no rules, mostly due to the influence of [Art Davie](/wiki/Art_Davie "Art Davie") and [Rorion Gracie](/wiki/Rorion_Gracie "Rorion Gracie") attempting to replicate mixed contests that existed in Brazil[[10]](#cite_note-11) and Japan. They would later implement a different set of rules (example: eliminating kicking a grounded opponent), which differed from other leagues which were more in favour of realistic, "street-like" fights.[[11]](#cite_note-MixedMartialArts.com-12) The first documented use of the term *mixed martial arts* was in a review of [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1") by television critic [Howard Rosenberg](/wiki/Howard_Rosenberg "Howard Rosenberg") in 1993.

Originally promoted as a competition to find the most effective martial arts for real unarmed combat, competitors from different fighting styles were pitted against one another in contests with relatively few rules.[[12]](#cite_note-slate-13) Later, individual fighters incorporated multiple martial arts into their style. MMA promoters were pressured to adopt [additional rules](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_rules "Mixed martial arts rules") to increase competitors' safety, to comply with sport regulations and to broaden mainstream acceptance of the sport.[[13]](#cite_note-NY2000009-14) Following these changes, the sport has seen increased popularity with a [pay-per-view](/wiki/Pay-per-view "Pay-per-view") business that rivals [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling").[[14]](#cite_note-PPV-15)

## From History to Present Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: From History to Present Day")]

### Precursors of Modern MMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Precursors of Modern MMA")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Leitai.jpg/250px-Leitai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leitai.jpg)

A [Chinese martial artist](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts") preparing to throw his opponent during a [lei tai](/wiki/Lei_tai "Lei tai") contest in [ancient China](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China")

In [ancient China](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China"), combat sport appeared in the form of [Leitai](/wiki/Leitai "Leitai"), a no-holds-barred mixed combat sport that combined [Chinese martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts"), boxing and wrestling.[[15]](#cite_note-Brownell-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Xwres8.jpg/250px-Xwres8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xwres8.jpg)

[*The Pancrastinae*](/wiki/Wrestlers_(sculpture) "Wrestlers (sculpture)"): a statue portraying the [pancratium](/wiki/Pancratium "Pancratium"), an event which took place in the Roman [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum"). Even as late as the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), statues were put up in Rome and other cities to honor remarkable pankratiasts. This statue, now part of the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi") collection, is a Roman copy of a lost Greek original, circa 3rd century BC.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a5/Greek_Pankratists_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greek_Pankratists_2.jpg)

Ancient Greek pankratiasts fighting. This drawing is an early 20th century copy of a scene from a [Panathenaic amphora](/wiki/Panathenaic_amphora "Panathenaic amphora").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), there was a sport called [pankration](/wiki/Pankration "Pankration"), which featured grappling and striking skills similar to those found in modern MMA. Pankration was formed by combining the already established wrestling and boxing traditions and, in Olympic terms, first featured in the 33rd [Olympiad](/wiki/Ancient_Olympic_Games "Ancient Olympic Games") in 648 BC. All strikes and holds were allowed with the exception of biting and gouging, which were banned. The fighters, called pankratiasts, fought until someone could not continue or signaled submission by raising their index finger; there were no rounds.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) According to the historian E. Norman Gardiner, "No branch of athletics was more popular than the pankration."[[19]](#cite_note-20) There is also evidence of similar mixed combat sports in [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), [India](/wiki/Indian_martial_arts "Indian martial arts") and [Japan](/wiki/Japanese_martial_arts "Japanese martial arts").[[15]](#cite_note-Brownell-16)

The mid-19th century saw the prominence of the new sport [savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate") in the combat sports circle. French savate fighters wanted to test their techniques against the traditional combat styles of its time. In 1852, a contest was held in France between French *savateurs* and English [bare-knuckle boxers](/wiki/Bare-knuckle_boxing "Bare-knuckle boxing"), in which French fighter Rambaud alias *la Resistance* fought English fighter Dickinson and won using his kicks. However, the English team still won the four other match-ups during the contest.[[20]](#cite_note-MAW-21) Contests occurred in the late 19th to mid-20th century between French *savateurs* and other combat styles. Examples include a 1905 fight between French savateur Georges Dubois and [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") practitioner Ernest "Re-nier" Regnier, which resulted in the latter winning by submission with an [armbar](/wiki/Armlock "Armlock"), as well as the highly publicized 1957 fight between French savateur and [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") Jacques Cayron and a young Japanese [karateka](/wiki/Karateka "Karateka") named Hiroo Mochizuki, which ended when Cayron knocked Hiroo out with a [hook](/wiki/Hook_(boxing) "Hook (boxing)").[[20]](#cite_note-MAW-21)

[Catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") appeared in the late 19th century, combining several global styles of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), including Indian [pehlwani](/wiki/Pehlwani "Pehlwani") and English wrestling.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) In turn, catch wrestling went on to greatly influence modern MMA.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[23]](#cite_note-24) No-holds-barred fighting reportedly took place in the late 1880s when wrestlers representing the style of catch wrestling and many others met in tournaments and music-hall challenge matches throughout Europe. In the US, the first major encounter between a boxer and a wrestler in modern times took place in 1887 when [John L. Sullivan](/wiki/John_L._Sullivan "John L. Sullivan"), then heavyweight world boxing champion, entered the ring with his trainer, wrestling champion [William Muldoon](/wiki/William_Muldoon "William Muldoon"), and was slammed to the mat in two minutes. The next publicized encounter occurred in the late 1890s when future heavyweight boxing champion [Bob Fitzsimmons](/wiki/Bob_Fitzsimmons "Bob Fitzsimmons") took on European wrestling champion [Ernest Roeber](/wiki/Ernest_Roeber "Ernest Roeber"). In September 1901, [Frank "Paddy" Slavin](/wiki/Frank_%22Paddy%22_Slavin "Frank \"Paddy\" Slavin"), who had been a contender for Sullivan's boxing title, knocked out future world wrestling champion [Frank Gotch](/wiki/Frank_Gotch "Frank Gotch") in [Dawson City](/wiki/Dawson_City "Dawson City"), Canada.[[24]](#cite_note-25) The judo-practitioner Ren-nierand, who gained fame after defeating George Dubois, would fight again in another similar contest, which he lost to Ukrainian Catch wrestler [Ivan Poddubny](/wiki/Ivan_Poddubny "Ivan Poddubny").[[20]](#cite_note-MAW-21)

Another early example of mixed martial arts was [Bartitsu](/wiki/Bartitsu "Bartitsu"), which [Edward William Barton-Wright](/wiki/Edward_William_Barton-Wright "Edward William Barton-Wright") founded in London in 1899. Combining [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate"), [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu") and [canne de combat](/wiki/Canne_de_combat "Canne de combat") (French stick fighting), Bartitsu was the first martial art known to have combined Asian and European fighting styles,[[25]](#cite_note-bart-26) and which saw MMA-style contests throughout England, pitting European catch wrestlers and Japanese judoka champions against representatives of various European wrestling styles.[[25]](#cite_note-bart-26)

Among the precursors of modern MMA are mixed style contests throughout Europe, Japan, and the Pacific Rim during the early 1900s.[[26]](#cite_note-27) In Japan, these contests were known as *merikan*, from the Japanese slang for "American [fighting]". *Merikan* contests were fought under a variety of rules, including points decision, best of three throws or knockdowns, and victory via knockout or submission.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

In 1909, [Erich Rahn](/wiki/Erich_Rahn "Erich Rahn") opened a [Tsutsumi Hōzan-ryū](/wiki/Tsutsumi_H%C5%8Dzan-ry%C5%AB "Tsutsumi Hōzan-ryū") jujutsu school in Germany where he mixed his style with boxing and wrestling.[[28]](#cite_note-29) [Sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)"), a martial art and combat sport developed in Russia in the early 1920s, merged various forms of combat styles such as wrestling, judo and striking into one unique martial art.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) In 1925, the founder of judo, [Jigoro Kano](/wiki/Kan%C5%8D_Jigor%C5%8D "Kanō Jigorō"), also created a form of [physical education](/wiki/Physical_education "Physical education") that included a combative form based in both judo and karate moves.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The popularity of professional wrestling, which was contested under various [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") rules at the time, waned after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), when the sport split into two genres: "[shoot](/wiki/Shoot_(professional_wrestling) "Shoot (professional wrestling)")", in which the fighters actually competed, and "[show](/wiki/Work_(professional_wrestling) "Work (professional wrestling)")", which evolved into modern [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling").[[32]](#cite_note-willoughby-33) In 1936, heavyweight boxing contender [Kingfish Levinsky](/wiki/Kingfish_Levinsky "Kingfish Levinsky") and professional wrestler [Ray Steele](/wiki/Ray_Steele_(US_wrestler) "Ray Steele (US wrestler)") competed in a mixed match, which catch wrestler Steele won in 35 seconds.[[32]](#cite_note-willoughby-33) 27 years later, Ray Steele's protégé [Lou Thesz](/wiki/Lou_Thesz "Lou Thesz") fought boxer [Jersey Joe Walcott](/wiki/Jersey_Joe_Walcott "Jersey Joe Walcott") twice in mixed style bouts. The first match was a real contest which Thesz won while the second match was a work, which Thesz also won.

In the 1940s in the Palama Settlement in Hawaii, five martial arts masters, under the leadership of [Adriano Emperado](/wiki/Adriano_Directo_Emperado "Adriano Directo Emperado"), curious to determine which martial art was best, began testing each other in their respective arts of kenpo, jujitsu, Chinese and American boxing and [tang soo do](/wiki/Tang_soo_do "Tang soo do"). From this they developed [kajukenbo](/wiki/Kajukenbo "Kajukenbo"), the first American mixed martial arts. Also in 1941, 7th dan judoka Haruna Hoshizaki addressed the topic of fights between judokas and boxers, and instructed judokas to learn boxing to better engage them.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

On February 12, 1963, three karatekas from *Oyama dojo* ([kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin") later) went to the [Lumpinee Boxing Stadium](/wiki/Lumpinee_Boxing_Stadium "Lumpinee Boxing Stadium") in Thailand and fought against three Muay Thai fighters. The three karate fighters were [Tadashi Nakamura](/wiki/Tadashi_Nakamura_(martial_artist) "Tadashi Nakamura (martial artist)"), [Kenji Kurosaki](/wiki/Kenji_Kurosaki "Kenji Kurosaki") and Akio Fujihira (also known as Noboru Osawa),[[34]](#cite_note-8_Limbs-35) while the Thai fighters were Tan Saren, Haufi Lukkontai and Rawee Dechachai.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Japan won 2–1: Nakamura and Fujihira both knocked out their opponents with punches, with Fujihira making usage of [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") techniques to gain advantage over the bigger Lukkontai,[[36]](#cite_note-37) while Kurosaki was knocked out by elbows by Dechachai. Kurosaki was an instructor rather than a contender, and that he had stood in as a substitute for an absent chosen fighter. In June of the same year, karateka and future kickboxer [Tadashi Sawamura](/wiki/Tadashi_Sawamura "Tadashi Sawamura") faced top Thai fighter Samarn Sor Adisorn: Sawamura was knocked down sixteen times on his way to defeat, but he went on to incorporate what he learned in that fight in kickboxing tournaments.[[34]](#cite_note-8_Limbs-35) On December 2, 1963, a catch wrestler and judoka "[Judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo")" [Gene Lebell](/wiki/Gene_Lebell "Gene Lebell") fought professional boxer Milo Savage in a [no-holds-barred](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") match. Lebell won by [harai goshi](/wiki/Harai_goshi "Harai goshi") to [rear naked choke](/wiki/Rear_naked_choke "Rear naked choke"), leaving Savage unconscious. This was the first televised bout of mixed-style fighting in North America. The hometown crowd was so enraged that they began to boo and throw chairs at Lebell.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

In the mid-20th century, mixed martial arts contests emerged in Hong Kong's [street fighting](/wiki/Street_fighting "Street fighting") culture in the form of rooftop fights. During the early 20th century, there was an influx of migrants from mainland China, including [Chinese martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts") teachers who opened up martial arts schools in Hong Kong. In the mid-20th century, soaring [crime in Hong Kong](/wiki/Crime_in_Hong_Kong "Crime in Hong Kong"), combined with limited [Hong Kong Police](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Police "Hong Kong Police") manpower, led to many young Hongkongers learning martial arts for [self-defence](/wiki/Self-defence "Self-defence"). Around the 1960s, there were about 400 martial arts schools in Hong Kong, teaching their own distinctive styles of martial arts. In Hong Kong's street fighting culture, there emerged a rooftop fight scene in the 1950s and 1960s, where gangs from rival martial arts schools challenged each other to bare-knuckle fights on Hong Kong's rooftops, in order to avoid crackdowns by colonial [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") authorities. The most famous fighter to emerge from Hong Kong's rooftop fight scene was [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee"), who combined different techniques from different martial arts schools into his own [hybrid martial arts](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") system called [Jeet Kune Do](/wiki/Jeet_Kune_Do "Jeet Kune Do"). Lee went on to popularize the concept of mixed martial arts internationally.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg/250px-Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg)

[Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") popularized the concept of mixed martial arts via his [hybrid philosophy](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") of [Jeet Kune Do](/wiki/Jeet_Kune_Do "Jeet Kune Do") during the late 1960s to early 1970s.

During the late 1960s to early 1970s, the concept of [hybrid martial arts](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") was popularized in the West by [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") via his system of [Jeet Kune Do](/wiki/Jeet_Kune_Do "Jeet Kune Do").[[39]](#cite_note-40) Lee believed that "the best fighter is not a boxer, karate or judo man. The best fighter is someone who can adapt to any style, to be formless, to adopt an individual's own style and not following the system of styles."[[40]](#cite_note-41) In 2004, UFC President [Dana White](/wiki/Dana_White "Dana White") would call Lee the "father of mixed martial arts" stating: "If you look at the way Bruce Lee trained, the way he fought, and many of the things he wrote, he said the perfect style was no style. You take a little something from everything. You take the good things from every different discipline, use what works, and you throw the rest away".[[41]](#cite_note-42)

A contemporary of Bruce Lee, [Wing Chun](/wiki/Wing_Chun "Wing Chun") practitioner [Wong Shun-leung](/wiki/Wong_Shun-leung "Wong Shun-leung"), gained prominence fighting in 60–100 illegal *[beimo](/wiki/Street_fighting "Street fighting")* fights against other Chinese martial artists of various styles. Wong also fought and won against Western fighters of other combat styles, such as his match against Russian boxer Giko,[[42]](#cite_note-PetersonPeterboro-43) his televised fight against a [fencer](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing"),[[43]](#cite_note-LamLeBlanc-44) and his fight against Taiwanese kung fu master Wu Ming-jeet.[[44]](#cite_note-Longley-45) Wong combined boxing and [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing") into his kung fu, as Bruce Lee did.

A fight between [Golden Gloves](/wiki/Golden_Gloves "Golden Gloves") boxing champion [Joey Hadley](/wiki/Cus_D%27Amato#Joey_Hadley "Cus D'Amato") and Arkansas Karate Champion David Valovich happened on June 22, 1976, at Memphis Blues Baseball Park. The bout had mixed rules: the [karateka](/wiki/Karateka "Karateka") was allowed to use his fists, feet and knees, while the boxer could only use his fists. Hadley won the fight via knockout on the first round.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In 1988, [Rick Roufus](/wiki/Rick_Roufus "Rick Roufus") challenged [Changpuek Kiatsongrit](/wiki/Changpuek_Kiatsongrit "Changpuek Kiatsongrit") to a non-title [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") vs. [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing") super fight. Roufus was at the time an undefeated Kickboxer and held both the [KICK](/wiki/Karate_International_Council_of_Kickboxing "Karate International Council of Kickboxing") Super Middleweight World title and the PKC Middleweight U.S. title. Kiatsongrit was finding it increasingly difficult to get fights in Thailand as his weight (70 kg) was not typical for Thailand, where competitive bouts tended to be at the lower weights. Roufus knocked Changpuek down twice with punches in the first round, breaking Changpuek's jaw, but lost by technical knockout in the fourth round due to the culmination of low kicks to the legs that he was unprepared for. This match was the first popular fight which showcased the power of such low kicks to a predominantly Western audience.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Regulated mixed martial arts competitions were first introduced in the United States by [CV Productions, Inc.](/wiki/CV_Productions,_Inc. "CV Productions, Inc.") Its first competition, called *[Tough Guy Contest](/wiki/Tough_Guy_Contest "Tough Guy Contest")* was held on March 20, 1980, [New Kensington, Pennsylvania](/wiki/New_Kensington,_Pennsylvania "New Kensington, Pennsylvania"), [Holiday Inn](/wiki/Holiday_Inn "Holiday Inn"). During that year the company renamed the brand to *Super Fighters* and sanctioned ten regulated tournaments in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). In 1983, Pennsylvania State Senate passed a bill known as the "[Tough Guy Law](/wiki/Tough_Guy_Law "Tough Guy Law")" that specifically called for: "Prohibiting Tough Guy contests or Battle of the Brawlers contests", and ended the sport.[[47]](#cite_note-NashJohn-48)[[48]](#cite_note-WernerSam-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)

### Modern MMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern MMA")]

Modern mixed martial arts began in two places: the [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo") events in Brazil in 1920s, and Japan's [Shoot wrestling](/wiki/Shoot_wrestling "Shoot wrestling") in 1970s. The modern form of Mixed Martial Arts had mainstream exposure in United States along with an initial international exposure when the [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family") created [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") in the early 90s. In the mid-90s, after the first event of [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC"), the sport went through a process of globalization, spreading to different countries. Finally, in the beginning of the 21st century, the modern MMA became a sport that enjoys worldwide presence.

Vale tudo began in the 1920s and became renowned through its association with the "[Gracie challenge](/wiki/Gracie_challenge "Gracie challenge")", which was issued by [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie") and [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") and upheld later by descendants of the [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family"). The "Gracie Challenges" were held in the garages and gyms of the Gracie family members. When the popularity grew, these types of mixed bouts were a staple attraction at the carnivals in Brazil.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_%28detail%29.jpg/250px-Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_%28detail%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_(detail).jpg)

[Panel](/wiki/Panel_(comics) "Panel (comics)") by Alfredo Storni featuring capoeirista [Ciríaco](/wiki/Francisco_da_Silva_Cir%C3%ADaco "Francisco da Silva Ciríaco") defeating jujitsu fighter [Sada Miyako](/wiki/Sada_Miyako "Sada Miyako") with a [rabo de arraia](/wiki/Rabo_de_arraia "Rabo de arraia") kick, *[O Malho](/wiki/O_Malho "O Malho")*, 1909.

The fight between [capoeirista](/wiki/Capoeirista "Capoeirista") [Francisco da Silva Ciríaco](/wiki/Francisco_da_Silva_Cir%C3%ADaco "Francisco da Silva Ciríaco") and jujitsu fighter [Sada Miyako](/wiki/Sada_Miyako "Sada Miyako") in Brazil stands as one of the earliest recorded interdisciplinary martial arts clashes in the country.[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)
In 1920, capoerista [Mestre Sinhozinho](/wiki/Agenor_Moreira_Sampaio "Agenor Moreira Sampaio") opened his martial arts school, where he taught *[capoeira carioca](/wiki/Capoeira_carioca "Capoeira carioca")* mixed with judo, wrestling and other styles.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Kimura_kesa.jpg/250px-Kimura_kesa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kimura_kesa.jpg)

[Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura_vs._H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie"), a 1951 bout between Japanese [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") fighter [Masahiko Kimura](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura "Masahiko Kimura") and [Brazilian jiu jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu_jitsu "Brazilian jiu jitsu") founder [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") in Brazil, was an early high-profile mixed martial arts bout.

The "[Gracie challenge](/wiki/Gracie_challenge "Gracie challenge")" at times used rules other than that of MMA. In 1951, a high-profile grappling match was [Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura_vs._H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Masahiko Kimura vs. Hélio Gracie"), which was wrestled between [judoka](/wiki/Judoka "Judoka") [Masahiko Kimura](/wiki/Masahiko_Kimura "Masahiko Kimura") and [Brazilian jiu jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu_jitsu "Brazilian jiu jitsu") founder [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") in Brazil. Kimura defeated Gracie using a [gyaku-ude-garami](/wiki/Kimura_lock "Kimura lock") armlock, which later became known as the "Kimura" in Brazilian jiu jitsu.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[Muhammad Ali vs. Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_vs._Antonio_Inoki "Muhammad Ali vs. Antonio Inoki") took place in Japan in 1976. The classic match-up between professional boxer and professional wrestler turned sour as each fighter refused to engage in the other's style, and after a 15-round stalemate it was declared a draw. [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") sustained a substantial amount of damage to his legs, as [Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Antonio_Inoki "Antonio Inoki") slide-kicked him continuously for the duration of the bout, causing him to be hospitalized for the next three days.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The fight played an important role in the history of mixed martial arts.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Japan had its own form of mixed martial arts discipline, [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto"), which evolved from shoot wrestling in 1985, as well as the shoot wrestling derivative [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase"), which was founded as a promotion in 1993. [Pancrase 1](/wiki/Pancrase:_Yes,_We_Are_Hybrid_Wrestlers_1 "Pancrase: Yes, We Are Hybrid Wrestlers 1") was held in Japan in September 1993, two months before [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1") was held in the United States in November 1993. The basis of modern mixed martial arts in Japan can be found in [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto"), a combat sport promotion founded by [Satoru Sayama](/wiki/Satoru_Sayama "Satoru Sayama") in 1989. Sayama was part of the [shoot-style](/wiki/Shoot_wrestling "Shoot wrestling") [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") movement, born in 1984 in the promotion [Universal Wrestling Federation](/wiki/Universal_Wrestling_Federation_(Japan) "Universal Wrestling Federation (Japan)"), that attempted to create a [combat](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport")-based style which blended [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing") and [submission grappling](/wiki/Submission_grappling "Submission grappling"). Another promotion formed around the same time by [Akira Maeda](/wiki/Akira_Maeda "Akira Maeda") called [Fighting Network RINGS](/wiki/Fighting_Network_RINGS "Fighting Network RINGS") initially started as a shoot-style professional wrestling promotion but it also promoted early mixed martial arts contests. From 1995 onwards it began identifying itself as a mixed martial arts promotion and moved away from the original shoot style. Professional wrestlers [Masakatsu Funaki](/wiki/Masakatsu_Funaki "Masakatsu Funaki") and [Minoru Suzuki](/wiki/Minoru_Suzuki "Minoru Suzuki") founded [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase") in 1993 which promoted legitimate contests initially under professional wrestling rules. These promotions inspired [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") which started in 1997. Pride was acquired by its rival Ultimate Fighting Championship in 2007.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)

In 1993, the sport was reintroduced to the United States by the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") (UFC).[[59]](#cite_note-Sánchez_Garcia,_R._2010-60) UFC promoters initially pitched the event as a real-life [fighting video game](/wiki/Fighting_video_game "Fighting video game") tournament similar to *[Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter "Street Fighter")* and *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat")*.[[60]](#cite_note-61) The sport gained international exposure and widespread publicity when jiu-jitsu fighter [Royce Gracie](/wiki/Royce_Gracie "Royce Gracie") won the [first Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1") tournament, submitting three challengers in a total of just five minutes.[[61]](#cite_note-62) sparking a revolution in martial arts.[[62]](#cite_note-peligro-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d4/Yukinakai-gordeau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yukinakai-gordeau.jpg)

[Yuki Nakai](/wiki/Yuki_Nakai "Yuki Nakai") fights [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1") finalist [Gerard Gordeau](/wiki/Gerard_Gordeau "Gerard Gordeau") at [Vale Tudo Japan 1995](/wiki/Vale_Tudo_Japan "Vale Tudo Japan").

Early mixed-match martial arts [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") bouts in Japan (known as *Ishu Kakutōgi Sen* (異種格闘技戦), literally "heterogeneous combat sports bouts") became popular with [Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Antonio_Inoki "Antonio Inoki") only in the 1970s. Inoki was a disciple of [Rikidōzan](/wiki/Rikid%C5%8Dzan "Rikidōzan"), but also of [Karl Gotch](/wiki/Karl_Gotch "Karl Gotch"), who trained numerous Japanese wrestlers in [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling").

The first [Vale Tudo Japan](/wiki/Vale_Tudo_Japan "Vale Tudo Japan") tournaments were held in 1994 and 1995 and were both won by [Rickson Gracie](/wiki/Rickson_Gracie "Rickson Gracie"). Around the same time, International Vale Tudo competition started to develop through (World Vale Tudo Championship (WVC), [VTJ](/wiki/VTJ "VTJ"), [IVC](/wiki/International_Vale_Tudo_Championship "International Vale Tudo Championship"), UVF etc.). Interest in mixed martial arts as a sport resulted in the creation of the [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") (Pride) in 1997.[[64]](#cite_note-whatispride-65)

The sport reached a new peak of popularity in North America in December 2006: a [rematch](/wiki/UFC_66 "UFC 66") between then [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") light heavyweight champion [Chuck Liddell](/wiki/Chuck_Liddell "Chuck Liddell") and former champion [Tito Ortiz](/wiki/Tito_Ortiz "Tito Ortiz"), rivaled the [PPV](/wiki/Pay-per-view "Pay-per-view") sales of some of the biggest boxing events of all time,[[65]](#cite_note-66) and helped the UFC's 2006 PPV gross surpass that of any promotion in PPV history. In 2007, [Zuffa LLC](/wiki/Zuffa "Zuffa"), the owners of the UFC MMA promotion, bought Japanese rival MMA brand [Pride FC](/wiki/Pride_FC "Pride FC"), merging the contracted fighters under one promotion.[[66]](#cite_note-67) Comparisons were drawn to the consolidation that occurred in other sports, such as the [AFL-NFL Merger](/wiki/AFL-NFL_Merger "AFL-NFL Merger") in [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football").[[67]](#cite_note-68)

#### Origin of the term *MMA*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origin of the term MMA")]

The first documented use of the name *mixed martial arts* was in a review of [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1") by television critic, [Howard Rosenberg](/wiki/Howard_Rosenberg "Howard Rosenberg"), in 1993.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) The term gained popularity when the website, newfullcontact.com (one of the biggest websites covering the sport at the time), hosted and reprinted the article. The first use of the term by a promotion was in September 1995 by Rick Blume, president and CEO of Battlecade Extreme Fighting, just after UFC 7.[[70]](#cite_note-71)
UFC official, [Jeff Blatnick](/wiki/Jeff_Blatnick "Jeff Blatnick"), was responsible for the Ultimate Fighting Championship officially adopting the name mixed martial arts. It was previously marketed as "Ultimate Fighting" and "No Holds Barred (NHB)", until Blatnick and [John McCarthy](/wiki/John_McCarthy_(mixed_martial_arts) "John McCarthy (mixed martial arts)") proposed the name "MMA" at the [UFC 17](/wiki/UFC_17 "UFC 17") rules meeting in response to increased public criticism.[[71]](#cite_note-72) The question as to who actually coined the name is still in debate.[[72]](#cite_note-KirikJenness-73)

#### Regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Regulation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Pentagon_Combat_2.png/250px-Pentagon_Combat_2.png)](/wiki/File:Pentagon_Combat_2.png)

A "[soccer kick](/wiki/Soccer_kick "Soccer kick")", movement where the fighter kicks the head of a downed opponent. While common in [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo"), early MMA and Japanese promotions, the soccer kick has been banned from the [Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Unified_Rules_of_Mixed_Martial_Arts "Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts").

The first state-regulated MMA event was held in Biloxi, Mississippi on August 23, 1996, with the sanctioning of IFC's Mayhem in Mississippi[[73]](#cite_note-74) show by the Mississippi Athletic Commission under William Lyons. The rules used were an adaptation of the kickboxing rules already accepted by most state athletic commissions. These modified kickboxing rules allowed for take downs and ground fighting and did away with rounds, although they did allow for fighters to be stood up by the referee and restarted if there was no action on the ground. These rules were the first in modern MMA to define fouls, fighting surfaces and the use of the cage.

In March 1997, the Iowa Athletic Commission officially sanctioned [Battlecade Extreme Fighting](/wiki/Battlecade_Extreme_Fighting "Battlecade Extreme Fighting") under a modified form of its existing rules for [Shootfighting](/wiki/Shootfighting "Shootfighting"). These rules created the three 'five-minute round/one-minute break' format, and mandated shootfighting gloves, as well as weight classes for the first time. Illegal blows were listed as groin strikes, head butting, biting, eye gouging, hair pulling, striking an opponent with an elbow while the opponent is on the mat, kidney strikes, and striking the back of the head with closed fist. Holding onto the ring or cage for any reason was defined as a foul.[[74]](#cite_note-EXTREME_FIGHTING_4_RULES_MEETING_GOES_ON_AS_PLANNED-75)[[75]](#cite_note-Iowa_Professional_Shoot_Fighting_Regulations-76) While there are minor differences between these and the final Unified Rules, notably regarding elbow strikes, the Iowa rules allowed mixed martial arts promoters to conduct essentially modern events legally, anywhere in the state. On March 28, 1997, Extreme Fighting 4 was held under these rules, making it the first show conducted under a version of the modern rules.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In April 2000, the [California State Athletic Commission](/wiki/California_State_Athletic_Commission "California State Athletic Commission") voted unanimously in favor of regulations that later became the foundation for the [Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Unified_Rules_of_Mixed_Martial_Arts "Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts"). However, when the legislation was sent to the California capital in Sacramento for review, it was determined that the sport fell outside the jurisdiction of the CSAC, rendering the vote meaningless.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

On September 30, 2000, the [New Jersey State Athletic Control Board](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Athletic_Control_Board "New Jersey State Athletic Control Board") (NJSACB) began allowing mixed martial arts promoters to conduct events in New Jersey. The first event was an IFC event titled Battleground 2000 held in Atlantic City. The intent was to allow the NJSACB to observe actual events and gather information to establish a comprehensive set of rules to regulate the sport effectively.[[77]](#cite_note-njacb-78)

On April 3, 2001, the NJSACB held a meeting to discuss the regulation of mixed martial arts events. This meeting attempted to unify the myriad rules and regulations which had been utilized by the different mixed martial arts organizations. At this meeting, the proposed uniform rules were agreed upon by the NJSACB, several other regulatory bodies, numerous promoters of mixed martial arts events and other interested parties in attendance. At the conclusion of the meeting, all parties in attendance were able to agree upon a uniform set of rules to govern the sport of mixed martial arts.[[77]](#cite_note-njacb-78)

The rules adopted by the NJSACB have become the de facto standard set of rules for professional mixed martial arts across North America. On July 30, 2009, a motion was made at the annual meeting of the [Association of Boxing Commissions](/wiki/Association_of_Boxing_Commissions "Association of Boxing Commissions") to adopt these rules as the "Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts". The motion passed unanimously.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In November 2005, the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") began to sanction mixed martial arts with the first annual Army Combatives Championships held by the [US Army Combatives School](/wiki/US_Army_Combatives_School "US Army Combatives School").[[79]](#cite_note-80)

[Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") formally decriminalized mixed martial arts with a vote on Bill S-209 on June 5, 2013. The bill allows for provinces to have the power to create athletic commissions to regulate and sanction professional mixed martial arts bouts.[[80]](#cite_note-CANL1-81)

#### Globalization, Worldwide Presence, and Popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Globalization, Worldwide Presence, and Popularity")]

The event, [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1"), became the beginning of the international exposure of this sport. Since then, the growth of mixed martial arts (MMA) globally has been remarkable over the past few decades. MMA has evolved from being a niche combat sport to one of the most popular and commercially successful sports worldwide.[[81]](#cite_note-82) In the mid-90s following [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1"), [Frederico Lapenda](/wiki/Frederico_Lapenda "Frederico Lapenda"), a film producer and fight promoter, helped the modern MMA expand further internationally, thus playing a pivotal role in the process of the sport's globalization.[[82]](#cite_note-83) [Dana White](/wiki/Dana_White "Dana White") became the CEO of [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") in 2001 and brought success to the promotion. This caused the modern MMA to become present worldwide from the early 21st century and afterwards.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

## MMA organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: MMA organizations")]

### Promotions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Promotions")]

Since the [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") came to prominence in [mainstream media](/wiki/Mainstream_media "Mainstream media") in 2006, and with their 2007 merger with [Pride FC](/wiki/Pride_FC "Pride FC") and purchases of [WEC](/wiki/World_Extreme_Cagefighting "World Extreme Cagefighting") and [Strikeforce](/wiki/Strikeforce_(mixed_martial_arts) "Strikeforce (mixed martial arts)"), it has been the most significant MMA promotion in the world in terms of popularity, salaries, [talent](#Fighter_ranking), and level of competition.

According to [Fight Matrix](/wiki/Fight_Matrix "Fight Matrix"), these are the promotions with the top ranked talent as of November 2024:[[84]](#cite_note-85)

1. [Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC)](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship_(UFC) "Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC)"). Based in Las Vegas, United States. Broadcasts their fights locally on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") (prior to 2019 on [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports "Fox Sports")) and on other networks around the world.
2. [Professional Fighters League](/wiki/Professional_Fighters_League "Professional Fighters League") (PFL). Based in McLean, Virginia. Broadcasts their fights locally on ESPN and ESPN+ and streaming internationally on [DAZN](/wiki/DAZN "DAZN") and other platforms
3. [Absolute Championship Akhmat](/wiki/Absolute_Championship_Akhmat "Absolute Championship Akhmat") (ACA). Based in [Grozny](/wiki/Grozny "Grozny"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). Broadcasts their fights on locally on [Match TV](/wiki/Match_TV "Match TV"), and other platforms around the world.
4. [Konfrontacja Sztuk Walki](/wiki/Konfrontacja_Sztuk_Walki "Konfrontacja Sztuk Walki") (KSW). Based in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") Poland. Broadcasts their fights locally on the [Polsat Sport 1](/wiki/Polsat_Sport_1 "Polsat Sport 1") and other networks around the world.
5. [ONE Championship](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship"). Based in Kallang, Singapore. Broadcasts their fights regionally on [Fox Sports Asia](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Asia "Fox Sports Asia") and streaming on their [Mobile app](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") (without [Geo-blocking](/wiki/Geo-blocking "Geo-blocking")).
6. [Oktagon MMA](/wiki/Oktagon_MMA "Oktagon MMA"). Based in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia").
7. [Legacy Fighting Alliance](/wiki/Legacy_Fighting_Alliance "Legacy Fighting Alliance") (LFA). based in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), Texas. Broadcasts their fights on [UFC Fight Pass](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Pass "UFC Fight Pass").

### Gyms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gyms")]

Main article: [List of professional MMA training camps](/wiki/List_of_professional_MMA_training_camps "List of professional MMA training camps")

There are hundreds of MMA training facilities throughout the world.[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)

MMA gyms serve as specialized training centers where fighters develop their skills across various martial arts disciplines, such as Brazilian jiu-jitsu, wrestling, Muay Thai, and boxing. These gyms provide structured environments for athletes to prepare for competition, offering coaching, sparring, and conditioning programs. Certain gyms, such as the [UFC Performance Institute](/wiki/UFC_Performance_Institute "UFC Performance Institute") offer facilities like [cryotherapy chambers](/wiki/Cryotherapy#Cryotherapy_chamber "Cryotherapy"), [underwater treadmills](/wiki/Treadmill#Underwater_treadmill "Treadmill"), and [DEXA](/wiki/DEXA "DEXA") machines.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The following are popular MMA gyms along with notable fighters that have trained out of them.

### Fighter development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fighter development")]

As a result of an increased number of competitors, organized training camps, information sharing, and modern [kinesiology](/wiki/Kinesiology "Kinesiology"), the understanding of the effectiveness of various strategies has been greatly improved. UFC commentator [Joe Rogan](/wiki/Joe_Rogan "Joe Rogan") claimed that martial arts evolved more in the ten years following 1993 (the [first UFC event](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1")) than in the preceding 700 years combined.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

> "During his reign atop the sport in the late 1990s he was the prototype – he could strike with the best strikers; he could grapple with the best grapplers; his endurance was second to none. "

— Mike Sloan describing UFC champion [Frank Shamrock](/wiki/Frank_Shamrock "Frank Shamrock")'s early dominance[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The high profile of modern MMA promotions such as UFC and Pride has fostered an accelerated development of the sport. The early 1990s saw a wide variety of traditional styles competing in the sport.[[90]](#cite_note-91) However, early competition saw varying levels of success among disparate styles.
In the early 1990s, practitioners of grappling based styles such as [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") dominated competition in the United States. Practitioners of striking based arts such as [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing"), and [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), who were unfamiliar with submission grappling, proved to be unprepared to deal with its [submission techniques](/wiki/Submission_hold "Submission hold").[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-UFCHISTORYHSW1-96) As competitions became more and more common, those with a base in striking arts became more competitive as they cross-trained in styles based around [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") and submission holds.[[95]](#cite_note-UFCHISTORYHSW1-96) Likewise, those from the varying grappling styles added striking techniques to their arsenal. This increase of [cross-training](/wiki/Cross-training "Cross-training") resulted in fighters becoming increasingly multidimensional and well-rounded in their skill-sets.

The new hybridization of fighting styles can be seen in the technique of "ground and pound" developed by wrestling-based UFC pioneers such as [Dan Severn](/wiki/Dan_Severn "Dan Severn"), [Don Frye](/wiki/Don_Frye "Don Frye") and [Mark Coleman](/wiki/Mark_Coleman "Mark Coleman"). These wrestlers realized the need for the incorporation of strikes on the ground as well as on the feet, and incorporated ground striking into their grappling-based styles. Mark Coleman stated at [UFC 14](/wiki/UFC_14 "UFC 14") that his strategy was to "Ground him and pound him", which may be the first televised use of the term.

Since the late 1990s, both strikers and grapplers have been successful at MMA, although it is rare to see any fighter who is not schooled in both striking and grappling arts reach the highest levels of competition.

### Fighter ranking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fighter ranking")]

See also: [Sports rating system](/wiki/Sports_rating_system "Sports rating system")

MMA fighters are ranked according to their performance and outcome of their fights and level of competition they faced. The most popular and used, ranking portals are:

* [Fight Matrix](/wiki/Fight_Matrix "Fight Matrix"): Ranks up to 250–500 fighters worldwide for every possible division male and female.
* [Sherdog](/wiki/Sherdog "Sherdog"): Ranks top 10 fighters worldwide only for current available UFC divisions. Also used by ESPN.
* [SB Nation](/wiki/SB_Nation "SB Nation"): Ranks top 14 fighters worldwide only for male divisions. Also used by *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*.
* [MMAjunkie.com](/wiki/MMAjunkie.com "MMAjunkie.com"): Ranks top 10 fighters worldwide for current UFC available divisions.
* [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC"): Ranks top 15 contenders, UFC signed fighters only, as per UFC divisions (for example: #2 means the fighter is #3 for the UFC, behind the Champion and the #1).
* Tapology: Ranks top 10 fighters worldwide for every possible division.[[96]](#cite_note-97)
* Ranking MMA: Top 50 MMA World Rankings for all Men's Divisions and Top 25 MMA World Rankings for all Women's Divisions. RankingMMA publishes Independent Mixed Martial Arts rankings that does not exclude any fighter based on their promotion. RankingMMA also provides UFC Rankings (Complete Roster), Historical MMA Rankings, Non-UFC Rankings, and MMA Prospect Rankings. Ranking MMA has published MMA World Rankings since 2006.
* *[Sports Illustrated](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated "Sports Illustrated")*: Ranks top 10 fighters worldwide for current UFC available divisions.[[97]](#cite_note-98)
* MMA Rising: Ranks top 10 fighters worldwide in every possible division.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Notable for their *Unified Women's Mixed Martial Arts. Rankings*[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101)
* MMA Weekly: Ranks top 10 male fighters worldwide in every possible division, and P4P for female fighters.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Also used by [Yahoo! Sports](/wiki/Yahoo!_Sports "Yahoo! Sports").

## Rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rules")]

Main article: [Mixed martial arts rules](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_rules "Mixed martial arts rules")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Osaka_Fight_Gear_Pro_MMA_Gloves.jpg/250px-Osaka_Fight_Gear_Pro_MMA_Gloves.jpg)](/wiki/File:Osaka_Fight_Gear_Pro_MMA_Gloves.jpg)

[MMA gloves](/wiki/MMA_gloves "MMA gloves"). They are fingerless gloves which allow both striking and grappling to occur.

The rules for modern mixed martial arts competitions have changed significantly since the early days of [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo"), Japanese [shoot wrestling](/wiki/Shoot_wrestling "Shoot wrestling"), and [UFC 1](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1"), and even more from the historic style of [pankration](/wiki/Pankration "Pankration"). As the knowledge of fighting techniques spread among fighters and spectators, it became clear that the original minimalist rule systems needed to be amended.[[102]](#cite_note-Warriors-103)
The main motivations for these rule changes were protection of the health of the fighters, the desire to shed the perception of "barbarism and lawlessness", and to be recognized as a legitimate sport.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The new rules included the introduction of [weight classes](/wiki/MMA_weight_classes "MMA weight classes"); as knowledge about submissions spread, differences in weight had become a significant factor. There are nine different weight classes in the [Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Unified_Rules_of_Mixed_Martial_Arts "Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts"). These nine weight classes include [flyweight](/wiki/Flyweight_(MMA) "Flyweight (MMA)") (up to 125 lb / 56.7 kg), [bantamweight](/wiki/Bantamweight_(MMA) "Bantamweight (MMA)") (up to 135 lb / 61.2 kg), [featherweight](/wiki/Featherweight_(MMA) "Featherweight (MMA)") (up to 145 lb / 65.8 kg), [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight_(MMA) "Lightweight (MMA)") (up to 155 lb / 70.3 kg), [welterweight](/wiki/Welterweight_(MMA) "Welterweight (MMA)") (up to 170 lb / 77.1 kg), [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight_(MMA) "Middleweight (MMA)") (up to 185 lb / 83.9 kg), [light heavyweight](/wiki/Light_heavyweight_(MMA) "Light heavyweight (MMA)") (up to 205 lb / 93.0 kg), [heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)") (up to 265 lb / 120.2 kg), and [super heavyweight](/wiki/Super_heavyweight_(MMA) "Super heavyweight (MMA)") with no upper weight limit.[[77]](#cite_note-njacb-78)

[Small, open-fingered gloves](/wiki/MMA_gloves "MMA gloves") were introduced to protect fists, reduce the occurrence of cuts (and stoppages due to cuts) and encourage fighters to use their hands for striking to allow more captivating matches. Gloves were first made mandatory in Japan's [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") promotion and were later adopted by the UFC as it developed into a regulated sport. Most professional fights have the fighters wear 4 oz gloves, whereas some jurisdictions require amateurs to wear a slightly heavier 6 oz glove for more protection for the hands and wrists.

Time limits were established to avoid long fights with little action where competitors conserved their strength. Matches without time limits also complicated the airing of live events. The time limits in most professional fights are three 5 minute rounds, and championship fights are normally five 5-minute rounds. Similar motivations produced the "stand up" rule, where the referee can stand fighters up if it is perceived that both are resting on the ground or not advancing toward a dominant position.[[102]](#cite_note-Warriors-103)

In the United States, state athletic and boxing commissions have played a crucial role in the introduction of additional rules because they oversee MMA in a similar fashion to boxing. In Japan and most of Europe, there is no regulating authority over competitions, so these organizations have greater freedom in rule development and event structure.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Previously, Japanese-based organization [Pride Fighting Championships](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships") held an opening 10-minute round followed by two five-minute rounds. Stomps, [soccer kicks](/wiki/Soccer_kick "Soccer kick") and knees to the head of a grounded opponent are legal, but elbow strikes to the head are not.[[103]](#cite_note-104) This rule set is more predominant in the Asian-based organizations as opposed to European and American rules. More recently, Singapore-based organization [ONE Championship](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship") allows soccer kicks and knees to the head of a grounded opponent as well as elbow strikes to the head, but does not allow head stomps.[[104]](#cite_note-105) In 2016, ONE later banned soccer kicks.[[105]](#cite_note-106) However, they still allow knees to the head of a grounded opponent. In 2024, the Association of Boxing Commissions and Combative Sports Mixed Martial Arts Committee made changes to the [Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Unified_Rules_of_Mixed_Martial_Arts "Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts"), loosening the rules surrounding what a grounded opponent is, meaning fighters are now more vulnerable to kicks and knees to the head, and they also eliminated the rules prohibiting "12 to 6" elbows.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

### Victory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Victory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/UFC_74_Respect_Bout%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-UFC_74_Respect_Bout%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UFC_74_Respect_Bout(cropped).jpg)

[UFC 74](/wiki/UFC_74 "UFC 74"); [Clay Guida](/wiki/Clay_Guida "Clay Guida") vs. [Marcus Aurelio](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelio "Marcus Aurelio")

Victory in a match is normally gained either by the judges' decision after an allotted amount of time has elapsed, a stoppage by the referee (for example if a competitor cannot defend themselves intelligently) or the fight doctor (due to an injury), a [submission](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sports) "Submission (combat sports)"), by a competitor's [cornerman](/wiki/Cornerman "Cornerman") throwing in the towel, or by [knockout](/wiki/Knockout "Knockout").

#### Knockout (KO)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Knockout (KO)")]

As soon as a fighter is unable to continue due to legal strikes, his opponent is declared the winner. As MMA rules allow submissions and [ground and pound](/wiki/Ground_and_pound "Ground and pound"), the fight is stopped to prevent further injury to the fighter.

#### Technical knockout (TKO)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Technical knockout (TKO)")]

**Referee stoppage:** The referee may stop a match in progress if:

* a fighter becomes dominant to the point where the opponent cannot intelligently defend themselves and is taking excessive damage as a result.
* a fighter appears to be losing consciousness as he/she is being struck.
* a fighter appears to have a significant injury such as a cut or a broken bone.

**Doctor stoppage/cut:** The referee will call for a time out if a fighter's ability to continue is in question as a result of apparent injuries, such as a large cut. The ring doctor will inspect the fighter and stop the match if the fighter is deemed unable to continue safely, rendering the opponent the winner. However, if the match is stopped as a result of an injury from illegal actions by the opponent, either a disqualification or no contest will be issued instead.

**Corner stoppage:** A fighter's corner may announce defeat on the fighter's behalf by throwing in the towel during the match in progress or between rounds. This is normally done when a fighter is being beaten to the point where it is dangerous and unnecessary to continue. In some cases, the fighter may be injured.

**Retirement:** A fighter is so dazed or exhausted that he/she cannot physically continue fighting. Usually occurs between rounds.

#### Submission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Submission")]

A fighter may admit defeat during a match by:

* A physical tap on the opponent's body or mat/floor.
* Tapping verbally.

**Technical Submission:** the referee stops the match when the fighter is caught in a submission hold and is in danger of being injured. This can occur when a fighter is choked unconscious, or when a bone has been broken in a submission hold (a broken arm due to a [kimura](/wiki/Armlock#Double_wristlock_(Gyaku_ude-garami) "Armlock"), etc.).

#### Decision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Decision")]

If the match goes the distance, then the outcome of the bout is determined by three judges. The judging criteria are organization-specific.

**Technical decision:** in the unified rules of MMA, if a fighter is unable to continue due to an accidental illegal technique late in the fight, a technical decision is rendered by the judges based on who is ahead on the judges' scorecards at that time. In a three-round fight, two rounds must be completed for a technical decision to be awarded and in a five-round fight, three rounds must be completed.

#### Other conditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other conditions")]

**Forfeit:** a fighter or their representative may forfeit a match prior to the beginning of the match, thereby losing the match.

**Disqualification:** a "warning" will be given when a fighter commits a foul or illegal action or does not follow the referee's instruction. Three warnings will result in a disqualification. Moreover, if a fighter is unable to continue due to a deliberate illegal technique from his opponent, the opponent will be disqualified.

**No contest:** in the event that both fighters commit a violation of the rules, or a fighter is unable to continue due to an injury from an accidental illegal technique, the match will be declared a "no contest", except in the case of a technical decision in the unified rules. A result can also be overturned to a no contest if the fighter that was originally victorious fails a post fight drug test for banned substances.

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Mixed martial arts promotions typically require that male fighters wear shorts in addition to being [barechested](/wiki/Barechested "Barechested"), thus precluding the use of [gi](/wiki/Keikogi "Keikogi") or fighting kimono to inhibit or assist [submission](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sport_term) "Submission (combat sport term)") holds. Male fighters are required by most athletic commissions to wear groin protectors underneath their trunks.[[77]](#cite_note-njacb-78) Female fighters wear [short shorts](/wiki/Short_shorts "Short shorts") and [sports bras](/wiki/Sports_bra "Sports bra") or other similarly snug-fitting tops. Both male and female fighters are required to wear a [mouthguard](/wiki/Mouthguard "Mouthguard").[[77]](#cite_note-njacb-78)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

The need for flexibility in the legs combined with durability prompted the creation of various fighting shorts brands, which then spawned a range of [mixed martial arts clothing](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_clothing "Mixed martial arts clothing") and casual wear available to the public.

### Fighting area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fighting area")]

See also: [Mixed martial arts cage](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_cage "Mixed martial arts cage")

According to the Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts, an MMA competition or exhibition may be held in a ring or a fenced area. The fenced area can be round[[108]](#cite_note-109) or have at least six sides. Cages vary: some replace the metal fencing with a net, others have a different shape from an octagon, as the term "The Octagon" is trademarked by the UFC (although the 8-sided shape itself is not trademarked).[[109]](#cite_note-110) The fenced area is called a cage generically, or a hexagon, an octagon or an octagon cage, depending on the shape.

* [![A ring used by PRIDE](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Fedor_vs_Coleman.JPG/500px-Fedor_vs_Coleman.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fedor_vs_Coleman.JPG "A ring used by PRIDE")

  A ring used by [PRIDE](/wiki/Pride_Fighting_Championships "Pride Fighting Championships")
* [![An octagon cage used by the UFC](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Chris_Weidman_knock_out_Anderson_Silva_at_UFC_162..jpg/960px-Chris_Weidman_knock_out_Anderson_Silva_at_UFC_162..jpg)](/wiki/File:Chris_Weidman_knock_out_Anderson_Silva_at_UFC_162..jpg "An octagon cage used by the UFC")

  An octagon cage used by the UFC

## Common disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Common disciplines")]

See also: [Mixed martial artists by style](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_by_style "Category:Mixed martial artists by style")

Most 'traditional' martial arts have a specific focus and these arts may be trained to improve in that area. Popular disciplines of each type include:[[110]](#cite_note-111)

* **[Stand-up](/wiki/Stand-up_fighting "Stand-up fighting")**: [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai"), [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"), [capoeira](/wiki/Capoeira "Capoeira"), [combat sambo](/wiki/Combat_sambo "Combat sambo"), [savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate") and [sanda](/wiki/Sanda_(sport) "Sanda (sport)") are trained to improve stand-up striking.
* **[Clinch](/wiki/Clinch_fighting "Clinch fighting")**: [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [freestyle wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling"), [folkstyle wrestling](/wiki/Folkstyle_wrestling "Folkstyle wrestling"), [Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling"), [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [sanda](/wiki/Sanda_(sport) "Sanda (sport)") and [sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)") are trained to improve [clinching](/wiki/Stand-up_grappling_position "Stand-up grappling position"), [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") and [throws](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)"), while [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") is trained to improve the [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") aspect of the clinch.
* **[Ground](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting")**: [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)"), [folkstyle wrestling](/wiki/Folkstyle_wrestling "Folkstyle wrestling"), [freestyle wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling"), [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling") and [luta livre](/wiki/Luta_livre "Luta livre") are trained to improve ground control and position, as well as to achieve [submission holds](/wiki/Submission_holds "Submission holds"), and defend against them.

Most styles have been adapted from their traditional forms, such as [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") stances, which lack effective counters to leg kicks, the [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") stance, which is poor for defending against takedowns due to its static nature and a light front leg, and [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") or [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") techniques, which must be adapted for no-gi competition and susceptibility to strikes. It is common for a fighter to train with multiple coaches of different styles or an organized fight team to improve various aspects of their game at once. [Cardiovascular conditioning](/wiki/Aerobic_exercise "Aerobic exercise"), speed drills, [strength training](/wiki/Strength_training "Strength training") and [flexibility](/wiki/Flexibility_(anatomy) "Flexibility (anatomy)") are also important aspects of a fighter's training. Some schools advertise their styles as simply "mixed martial arts", which has become a style in itself, but the training will still often be split into different sections.

While mixed martial arts was initially practiced almost exclusively by competitive fighters, this is no longer the case. As the sport has become more mainstream and more widely taught, it has become accessible to wider range of practitioners of all ages. Proponents of this sort of training argue that it is safe for anyone, of any age, with varying levels of competitiveness and fitness.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113)

### Brazilian jiu-jitsu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Brazilian jiu-jitsu")]

Main article: [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing Brazilian jiu-jitsu")

Brazilian jiu-jitsu (BJJ) is a form of [submission grappling](/wiki/Submission_grappling "Submission grappling"). It came to international prominence in the martial arts community in the early 1990s, when BJJ expert [Royce Gracie](/wiki/Royce_Gracie "Royce Gracie") won the [first](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1"), [second](/wiki/UFC_2 "UFC 2"), and [fourth](/wiki/UFC_4 "UFC 4") Ultimate Fighting Championships, which at the time were [single-elimination](/wiki/Single-elimination "Single-elimination") martial arts tournaments. Royce often fought successfully against larger opponents who practiced other styles, including [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), [shoot-fighting](/wiki/Shoot-fighting "Shoot-fighting"), [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"). It has since become a staple art and key component for many MMA fighters. BJJ is largely credited for bringing widespread attention to the importance of [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting"). BJJ is primarily a ground-based fighting style that applies close range [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling") techniques and uses [joint locks](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") and [chokeholds](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold") to submit the adversary. But standup techniques can also be used such as [throws](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)"), [holds](/wiki/Grappling_hold "Grappling hold"), and [strikes](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)").

Some notable fighters who are known for using BJJ skills include: [Alexandre Pantoja](/wiki/Alexandre_Pantoja "Alexandre Pantoja"), [Amanda Nunes](/wiki/Amanda_Nunes "Amanda Nunes"), [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva"), [Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rodrigo_Nogueira "Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira"), [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira"), [Cris Cyborg](/wiki/Cris_Cyborg "Cris Cyborg"), [Deiveson Figueiredo](/wiki/Deiveson_Figueiredo "Deiveson Figueiredo"), [Demian Maia](/wiki/Demian_Maia "Demian Maia"), [Fabrício Werdum](/wiki/Fabr%C3%ADcio_Werdum "Fabrício Werdum"), [Glover Teixeira](/wiki/Glover_Teixeira "Glover Teixeira"), [José Aldo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Aldo "José Aldo"), [Junior dos Santos](/wiki/Junior_dos_Santos "Junior dos Santos"), [Lyoto Machida](/wiki/Lyoto_Machida "Lyoto Machida"), [Maurício 'Shogun' Rua](/wiki/Maur%C3%ADcio_Rua "Maurício Rua"), [Rafael dos Anjos](/wiki/Rafael_dos_Anjos "Rafael dos Anjos"), [Ricardo Arona](/wiki/Ricardo_Arona "Ricardo Arona"), [Ronaldo Souza](/wiki/Ronaldo_Souza "Ronaldo Souza"), [Vitor Belfort](/wiki/Vitor_Belfort "Vitor Belfort"), [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva"), [Mackenzie Dern](/wiki/Mackenzie_Dern "Mackenzie Dern"), [Aljamain Sterling](/wiki/Aljamain_Sterling "Aljamain Sterling"), [B.J. Penn](/wiki/B.J._Penn "B.J. Penn"), [Brian Ortega](/wiki/Brian_Ortega "Brian Ortega"), [Brandon Moreno](/wiki/Brandon_Moreno "Brandon Moreno"), [Chael Sonnen](/wiki/Chael_Sonnen "Chael Sonnen"), [Demetrious Johnson](/wiki/Demetrious_Johnson "Demetrious Johnson"), [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir"), [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre"), [José Alberto Quiñónez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Alberto_Qui%C3%B1%C3%B3nez "José Alberto Quiñónez"), [Gerald Meerschaert](/wiki/Gerald_Meerschaert "Gerald Meerschaert"), [Ilia Topuria](/wiki/Ilia_Topuria "Ilia Topuria"), [Jim Miller](/wiki/Jim_Miller_(fighter) "Jim Miller (fighter)"), [Nate Diaz](/wiki/Nate_Diaz "Nate Diaz"), [Gabriel Benítez](/wiki/Gabriel_Ben%C3%ADtez "Gabriel Benítez"), [Tom Aspinall](/wiki/Tom_Aspinall "Tom Aspinall"), and [Tony Ferguson](/wiki/Tony_Ferguson "Tony Ferguson").

### Wrestling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Wrestling")]

Main article: [Wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing wrestling](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_wrestling "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing wrestling")

Wrestling (including [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling"), [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling") and American [folkstyle](/wiki/Folkstyle "Folkstyle")) gained tremendous respect due to its effectiveness in mixed martial arts competitions. It is widely studied by mixed martial artists as Wrestling allows competitors to control where the match will go: superior wrestlers can dominate the [Clinch](/wiki/Clinching_position "Clinching position") and take their opponents into the ground with its excellent [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)"), particularly against the legs, where they will transition into [groundfighting](/wiki/Groundfighting "Groundfighting") and can either get a superior top position and start striking their opponent (a tactic known as G*round-and-Pound*)[[113]](#cite_note-Yahoo-2009-114) or start grappling for submissions. While wrestlers with stronger striking base can use defensive wrestling to defend takedowns maintain the fight in the feet where they use their superior striking, a tactic known as "[*Sprawl*](/wiki/Sprawl_(grappling) "Sprawl (grappling)")*-and-Brawl*",[[114]](#cite_note-Kendall_Shields-2010-115) or use wrestling to escape submission attempts. It is also credited for conferring an emphasis on conditioning for explosive movement and stamina, both of which are critical in competitive mixed martial arts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Mma_ground_fighting.jpg/250px-Mma_ground_fighting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mma_ground_fighting.jpg)

[Groundfighting](/wiki/Groundfighting "Groundfighting") in MMA. Differently from other [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling")-based martial arts, you are allowed to strike your opponent when fighting on the ground.

There are multiple wrestling styles around the world which MMA fighters have as their base. American fighters are usually trained in [folkstyle wrestling](/wiki/Folkstyle_wrestling "Folkstyle wrestling"), the style competed in high school and college competitions. Many American champions were former [NCAA Division I Wrestling Champions](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Wrestling_Championships "NCAA Division I Wrestling Championships"), such as [Kevin Randleman](/wiki/Kevin_Randleman "Kevin Randleman") and [Mark Kerr](/wiki/Mark_Kerr_(fighter) "Mark Kerr (fighter)"). While fighters from around the world train primarily in "international" Olympic styles such as [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling") and [Freestyle wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling"). Some former [wrestlers who competed in the Olympics](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Wrestling at the Summer Olympics") have joined MMA competition, such as [Daniel Cormier](/wiki/Daniel_Cormier "Daniel Cormier"), [Dan Henderson](/wiki/Dan_Henderson "Dan Henderson"), [Ben Askren](/wiki/Ben_Askren "Ben Askren"), silver medalists [Matt Lindland](/wiki/Matt_Lindland "Matt Lindland") and [Yoel Romero](/wiki/Yoel_Romero "Yoel Romero"), and gold medalist [Henry Cejudo](/wiki/Henry_Cejudo "Henry Cejudo").[[115]](#cite_note-116) Some fighters have also come from local [Folk wrestling](/wiki/Folk_wrestling "Folk wrestling") backgrounds, UFC flyweight champion [Deiveson Figueiredo](/wiki/Deiveson_Figueiredo "Deiveson Figueiredo") is trained at *Luta Marajoara*, a folk wrestling style from [Marajó island](/wiki/Maraj%C3%B3_island "Marajó island").[[116]](#cite_note-117)
Notable wrestlers who were MMA competitors include: [Khabib Nurmagomedov](/wiki/Khabib_Nurmagomedov "Khabib Nurmagomedov"), [Jon Jones](/wiki/Jon_Jones "Jon Jones"), [Chael Sonnen](/wiki/Chael_Sonnen "Chael Sonnen"), [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre"), [Cain Velasquez](/wiki/Cain_Velasquez "Cain Velasquez"), [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture"), [Brock Lesnar](/wiki/Brock_Lesnar "Brock Lesnar"), [Mark Coleman](/wiki/Mark_Coleman "Mark Coleman"), [Frankie Edgar](/wiki/Frankie_Edgar "Frankie Edgar"), [Colby Covington](/wiki/Colby_Covington "Colby Covington"), [Kamaru Usman](/wiki/Kamaru_Usman "Kamaru Usman"), [Justin Gaethje](/wiki/Justin_Gaethje "Justin Gaethje"), [Chris Weidman](/wiki/Chris_Weidman "Chris Weidman"), [Daniel Cormier](/wiki/Daniel_Cormier "Daniel Cormier"), [Dan Henderson](/wiki/Dan_Henderson "Dan Henderson"), [Tito Ortiz](/wiki/Tito_Ortiz "Tito Ortiz"), [Khamzat Chimaev](/wiki/Khamzat_Chimaev "Khamzat Chimaev"), [Ilia Topuria](/wiki/Ilia_Topuria "Ilia Topuria"), [Tyron Woodley](/wiki/Tyron_Woodley "Tyron Woodley"), [Yoel Romero](/wiki/Yoel_Romero "Yoel Romero"), [Deiveson Figueiredo](/wiki/Deiveson_Figueiredo "Deiveson Figueiredo"), [Michael Chandler](/wiki/Michael_Chandler "Michael Chandler") and [Henry Cejudo](/wiki/Henry_Cejudo "Henry Cejudo").

#### Greco-Roman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Greco-Roman")]

Main article: [Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_Greco-Roman_wrestling "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing Greco-Roman wrestling")

Greco-Roman wrestling is one of two styles of wrestling contested at the Olympic Games, the other being Freestyle. Greco-Roman wrestling only allows for holds above the waist and has a strong emphasis on clinch fighting. Due to the difficulty to achieve takedowns when one is not allowed to attack the legs, Greco-Roman is not utilized in MMA as often as styles that do allow fighters to attack the legs, such as Freestyle and Catch. Despite this, there have been fighters who come from a background in Greco-Roman wrestling.
