Notable examples are [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture"), [Dan Henderson](/wiki/Dan_Henderson "Dan Henderson"), [Mark Madsen](/wiki/Mark_Madsen_(fighter) "Mark Madsen (fighter)"), [Matt Lindland](/wiki/Matt_Lindland "Matt Lindland") (all four were Olympic wrestlers or Olympic alternates), [Jon Jones](/wiki/Jon_Jones "Jon Jones"), [Dan Severn](/wiki/Dan_Severn "Dan Severn"), [Ilia Topuria](/wiki/Ilia_Topuria "Ilia Topuria"), [Alexander Volkanovski](/wiki/Alexander_Volkanovski "Alexander Volkanovski"), [Magomed Ankalaev](/wiki/Magomed_Ankalaev "Magomed Ankalaev") and [Sergei Pavlovich](/wiki/Sergei_Pavlovich "Sergei Pavlovich").

#### Catch-as-catch-can

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Catch-as-catch-can")]

Main article: [Catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling")

See also: [Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing catch wrestling](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_catch_wrestling "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing catch wrestling")

[Catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") is the ancestor of freestyle wrestling and includes submissions which are prohibited in freestyle wrestling.[[117]](#cite_note-Catch-118) Widely popular around the world during the 19th and 20th centuries, catch wrestling underwent a decline as its amateur-side became olympic freestyle wrestling, while the professional side became modern [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling"). Catch survived in Japanese *[Puroresu](/wiki/Puroresu "Puroresu")*-style Pro Wrestling, where wrestlers such as [Antonio Inoki](/wiki/Antonio_Inoki "Antonio Inoki") and [Karl Gotch](/wiki/Karl_Gotch "Karl Gotch") promoted "strong style pro wrestling", that while [worked](/wiki/Work_(professional_wrestling) "Work (professional wrestling)"), had realistic and full contact moves, resulting in the creation of the [Universal Wrestling Federation](/wiki/Universal_Wrestling_Federation_(Japan) "Universal Wrestling Federation (Japan)") and [Shoot wrestling](/wiki/Shoot_wrestling "Shoot wrestling") (which in their own turn would inspire the creation of legit proto-MMA [shootfighting](/wiki/Shootfighting "Shootfighting") organizations such as [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") and [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase")). Many pro wrestlers that trained in shoot-style would later compete in MMA, which led to resurgence of Catch with the advent of mixed martial arts in the 90s. The term *no holds barred* was used originally to describe the wrestling method prevalent in catch wrestling tournaments during the late 19th century wherein no wrestling holds were banned from the competition, regardless of how dangerous they might be. The term was applied to mixed martial arts matches, especially at the advent of the Ultimate Fighting Championship.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

A lot of MMA fighters train in catch wrestling as their sole grappling style or as a complement to Brazilian jiu-jitsu, as it teaches techniques and tactics not found in Brazilian jiu-jitsu.[[117]](#cite_note-Catch-118) Notable MMA fighters who use catch wrestling as their primary grappling style include: [Josh Barnett](/wiki/Josh_Barnett "Josh Barnett"), [Ken Shamrock](/wiki/Ken_Shamrock "Ken Shamrock"), [Frank Shamrock](/wiki/Frank_Shamrock "Frank Shamrock"), [Bas Rutten](/wiki/Bas_Rutten "Bas Rutten"), [Kazushi Sakuraba](/wiki/Kazushi_Sakuraba "Kazushi Sakuraba"), [Erik Paulson](/wiki/Erik_Paulson "Erik Paulson"), [Bobby Lashley](/wiki/Bobby_Lashley "Bobby Lashley"), [Minoru Suzuki](/wiki/Minoru_Suzuki "Minoru Suzuki"), [Masakatsu Funaki](/wiki/Masakatsu_Funaki "Masakatsu Funaki"), [Rumina Sato](/wiki/Rumina_Sato "Rumina Sato"), [Masakazu Imanari](/wiki/Masakazu_Imanari "Masakazu Imanari"), [Muhammad Mokaev](/wiki/Muhammad_Mokaev "Muhammad Mokaev") and [Paul Sass](/wiki/Paul_Sass "Paul Sass").[[117]](#cite_note-Catch-118)

### Kickboxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Kickboxing")]

Main article: [Kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing Kickboxing](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_boxing "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing boxing")

[Kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing"), along with boxing, are recognised as a foundation for striking in mixed martial arts, and are both widely practiced and taught. Each has different techniques. Kickboxing is a group of stand-up combat martial arts based on kicking and punching. The modern style originated in Japan, developed from Karate, and had additional development in the Netherlands and the United States. Different governing bodies apply different rules, such as allowing the use of elbows, knees, clinching or throws, etc. Notable fighters include [Alex Pereira](/wiki/Alex_Pereira "Alex Pereira"), [Joanna Jędrzejczyk](/wiki/Joanna_J%C4%99drzejczyk "Joanna Jędrzejczyk"), [Zhang Weili](/wiki/Zhang_Weili "Zhang Weili"), [Edson Barboza](/wiki/Edson_Barboza "Edson Barboza"), [Darren Till](/wiki/Darren_Till "Darren Till"), [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva"), [José Aldo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Aldo "José Aldo"), [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira"), [Mauricio Rua](/wiki/Mauricio_Rua "Mauricio Rua"), [Wanderlei Silva](/wiki/Wanderlei_Silva "Wanderlei Silva"), [Ciryl Gane](/wiki/Ciryl_Gane "Ciryl Gane"), [Donald Cerrone](/wiki/Donald_Cerrone "Donald Cerrone"), [Jiří Procházka](/wiki/Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Proch%C3%A1zka "Jiří Procházka") [Cris Cyborg](/wiki/Cris_Cyborg "Cris Cyborg"), [Stephen Thompson](/wiki/Stephen_Thompson_(fighter) "Stephen Thompson (fighter)"), [Mirko Cro Cop](/wiki/Mirko_Cro_Cop "Mirko Cro Cop"), [Alistair Overeem](/wiki/Alistair_Overeem "Alistair Overeem"), [Israel Adesanya](/wiki/Israel_Adesanya "Israel Adesanya"), [Sean O'Malley](/wiki/Sean_O%27Malley_(fighter) "Sean O'Malley (fighter)"), [Michael Page](/wiki/Michael_Page "Michael Page"), [Yair Rodríguez](/wiki/Yair_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Yair Rodríguez"), [Cory Sandhagen](/wiki/Cory_Sandhagen "Cory Sandhagen"), [Alexander Volkanovski](/wiki/Alexander_Volkanovski "Alexander Volkanovski"), [Volkan Oezdemir](/wiki/Volkan_Oezdemir "Volkan Oezdemir"), [Dricus Du Plessis](/wiki/Dricus_Du_Plessis "Dricus Du Plessis") and [Leon Edwards](/wiki/Leon_Edwards "Leon Edwards").

### Boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Boxing")]

Main article: [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing boxing](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_boxing "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing boxing")

[Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") is a combat form that is widely used in MMA and is one of the primary striking bases for many fighters.[[119]](#cite_note-bloodyelbow.com-120) Boxing punches account for the vast majority of strikes during the stand up portion of a bout and also account for the largest number of significant strikes, knock downs and KOs in MMA matches.[[120]](#cite_note-121) Several aspects of boxing are extremely valuable such as footwork, combinations, and defensive techniques such as slips, head movement and stance (including chin protection and keeping hands up) commonly known as the Guard position.[[121]](#cite_note-122) Boxing-based fighters have also been shown to throw and land a higher volume of strikes when compared with other striking bases, at a rate of 3.88 per minute with 9.64 per minute thrown (compared with Muay Thai at 3.46 and 7.50, respectively).[[119]](#cite_note-bloodyelbow.com-120) Fighters known for using boxing include: [Petr Yan](/wiki/Petr_Yan "Petr Yan"), [Dustin Poirier](/wiki/Dustin_Poirier "Dustin Poirier"), [Conor McGregor](/wiki/Conor_McGregor "Conor McGregor"), [Max Holloway](/wiki/Max_Holloway "Max Holloway"), [Erik Pérez](/wiki/Erik_P%C3%A9rez "Erik Pérez"), [Calvin Kattar](/wiki/Calvin_Kattar "Calvin Kattar"), [Sean Strickland](/wiki/Sean_Strickland "Sean Strickland"), [Cain Velasquez](/wiki/Cain_Velasquez "Cain Velasquez"), [Nick Diaz](/wiki/Nick_Diaz "Nick Diaz"), [Glover Teixeira](/wiki/Glover_Teixeira "Glover Teixeira"), [José Aldo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Aldo "José Aldo"), [Ilia Topuria](/wiki/Ilia_Topuria "Ilia Topuria"), [Junior dos Santos](/wiki/Junior_dos_Santos "Junior dos Santos"), [B.J. Penn](/wiki/B.J._Penn "B.J. Penn"), [Dan Hardy](/wiki/Dan_Hardy "Dan Hardy"), [Shane Carwin](/wiki/Shane_Carwin "Shane Carwin"), [Jack Della Maddalena](/wiki/Jack_Della_Maddalena "Jack Della Maddalena"), [Francis Ngannou](/wiki/Francis_Ngannou "Francis Ngannou"), [Alexander Gustafsson](/wiki/Alexander_Gustafsson "Alexander Gustafsson"), [Manuel Torres](/wiki/Manuel_Torres_(fighter) "Manuel Torres (fighter)"), [Jamahal Hill](/wiki/Jamahal_Hill "Jamahal Hill"), [Justin Gaethje](/wiki/Justin_Gaethje "Justin Gaethje") and [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski").

### Luta Livre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Luta Livre")]

Main article: [Luta Livre](/wiki/Luta_Livre "Luta Livre")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing luta livre](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_Luta_Livre "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing Luta Livre")

[Luta Livre](/wiki/Luta_Livre "Luta Livre") (also referred to Luta Livre *Brasileira*, *Submission* or *Esportiva*) is a Brazilian [submission wrestling](/wiki/Submission_wrestling "Submission wrestling") style, developed in Brazil in the 1920s by [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") practitioner [Euclydes "Tatu" Hatem](/wiki/Euclydes_Hatem "Euclydes Hatem"), including techniques from [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling") and [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu"). In 1942, Hatem solidified the art's legitimacy by defeating George Gracie in a historic match.[[122]](#cite_note-123) Decades later, in 1968, his student [Euclides Pereira](/wiki/Euclides_Pereira "Euclides Pereira") — repeated the feat by defeating [Carlson Gracie](/wiki/Carlson_Gracie "Carlson Gracie").[[123]](#cite_note-124)

Luta livre is divided in the categories of *esportiva*, which is a form of submission grappling, differentiating from no-gi BJJ with its focus on quick and energetic submissions, and *[vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo")*, which includes strikes both standing up and on the ground in addition to grappling and submissions.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Luta livre was important to the development of mixed martial arts, as rivalry between jiu-jitsu and luta livre fueled the [vale tudo](/wiki/Vale_tudo "Vale tudo") scene. However, the success of Brazilian jiu-jitsu over luta livre practitioners, especially after the *Desafio: Jiu Jitsu vs Luta Livre* event in 1991 (which was broadcast live by [Rede Globo](/wiki/Rede_Globo "Rede Globo")), resulted in the style waning in popularity,[[125]](#cite_note-126) although it seems to be making a resurgence, especially as an alternative to both Brazilian jiu-jitsu and catch wrestling.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Some notable luta livre practitioners in MMA include: [Marco Ruas](/wiki/Marco_Ruas "Marco Ruas"), [Eugenio Tadeu](/wiki/Eugenio_Tadeu "Eugenio Tadeu"), [José Aldo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Aldo "José Aldo"), [Renato Sobral](/wiki/Renato_Sobral "Renato Sobral"), [Pedro Rizzo](/wiki/Pedro_Rizzo "Pedro Rizzo"), [Alexandre Franca Nogueira](/wiki/Alexandre_Franca_Nogueira "Alexandre Franca Nogueira"), [Terry Etim](/wiki/Terry_Etim "Terry Etim"), [Jesus Pinedo](/wiki/Jesus_Pinedo "Jesus Pinedo") and [Darren Till](/wiki/Darren_Till "Darren Till").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/FN_UCfight_keto.jpg/250px-FN_UCfight_keto.jpg)](/wiki/File:FN_UCfight_keto.jpg)

Fighter performs a [takedown](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") on his opponent.

### Judo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Judo")]

Main article: [Judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing judo](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_judo "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing judo")

Judo is a Japanese grappling martial art which has both *[ne-waza](/wiki/Ne-waza "Ne-waza")* (ground grappling) and *[tachi-waza](/wiki/Tachi-waza "Tachi-waza")* (standing grappling), several [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") practitioners have competed in mixed martial arts matches. They use their knowledge in judo for clinching and for doing explosive and fast takedowns which quickly transition into submission holds in the ground. However, judo is traditionally and exclusively trained using the [judogi](/wiki/Judogi "Judogi"), as such, many techniques and strategies from judo can not be translated into MMA.[[127]](#cite_note-128) Fighters who hold a black belt in judo include [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko"), [Marco Ruas](/wiki/Marco_Ruas "Marco Ruas"), [Khabib Nurmagomedov](/wiki/Khabib_Nurmagomedov "Khabib Nurmagomedov"), [Islam Makhachev](/wiki/Islam_Makhachev "Islam Makhachev"), [Ian Garry](/wiki/Ian_Garry "Ian Garry"), [Dong Hyun Kim](/wiki/Dong_Hyun_Kim "Dong Hyun Kim"), [Cub Swanson](/wiki/Cub_Swanson "Cub Swanson"), [Don Frye](/wiki/Don_Frye "Don Frye"), [Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Rodrigo_Nogueira "Antônio Rodrigo Nogueira"), [Fabricio Werdum](/wiki/Fabricio_Werdum "Fabricio Werdum"), [Vitor Belfort](/wiki/Vitor_Belfort "Vitor Belfort"), [Benoît Saint-Denis](/wiki/Beno%C3%AEt_Saint-Denis "Benoît Saint-Denis"), [Merab Dvalishvili](/wiki/Merab_Dvalishvili "Merab Dvalishvili"), [Reinier de Ridder](/wiki/Reinier_de_Ridder "Reinier de Ridder") and Olympian judokas [Ronda Rousey](/wiki/Ronda_Rousey "Ronda Rousey"),[[128]](#cite_note-129) [Hector Lombard](/wiki/Hector_Lombard "Hector Lombard"), [Rick Hawn](/wiki/Rick_Hawn "Rick Hawn")[[129]](#cite_note-130) and [Hidehiko Yoshida](/wiki/Hidehiko_Yoshida "Hidehiko Yoshida"). Former WEC middleweight champion [Paulo Filho](/wiki/Paulo_Filho "Paulo Filho") has credited judo for his success in an interview.[[130]](#cite_note-Paulo_Filho_Youtube_interview-131)

### Sambo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sambo")]

Main article: [Sambo (martial art)](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing sambo](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_sambo "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing sambo")

Sambo is a Russian martial art, combat sport and self-defense system.[[131]](#cite_note-132) It is a mixture of [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") and [freestyle wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling") using a [keikogi](/wiki/Keikogi "Keikogi") known as [kurtka](/wiki/Kurtka "Kurtka"). Sambo focuses on throwing, takedowns, grappling, and includes submissions from judo and [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"). Sports sambo is characterized as a grappling style focused in [pinning](/wiki/Pin_(amateur_wrestling) "Pin (amateur wrestling)") and in explosive takedowns which can be quickly transitioned into devastating [leglocks](/wiki/Leglock "Leglock"). Sambo also has a modality known as *combat sambo*, which adds punches, kicks, elbows and knees, making it a proto-MMA hybrid fighting style. Sambo is popular in Russia and eastern Europe, where it is taught as a complement to judo and wrestling training, Sambo also provides a good base for MMA with all-around skills for combining grappling and striking. Some notable Sambo fighters that transitioned into MMA include: [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko"), [Khabib Nurmagomedov](/wiki/Igor_Vovchanchyn "Igor Vovchanchyn"), [Islam Makhachev](/wiki/Islam_Makhachev "Islam Makhachev"), [Igor Vovchanchyn](/wiki/Igor_Vovchanchyn "Igor Vovchanchyn"), [Oleg Taktarov](/wiki/Oleg_Taktarov "Oleg Taktarov"), [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski"), [Yaroslav Amosov](/wiki/Yaroslav_Amosov "Yaroslav Amosov"), and [Shavkat Rakhmonov](/wiki/Shavkat_Rakhmonov "Shavkat Rakhmonov").

### Karate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Karate")]

Main article: [Karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing karate](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_karate "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing karate")

[Karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate") is a striking-based Japanese with [Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawa_Island "Okinawa Island") origins martial art using punches, kicks, sometimes elbows, knees and even limited grappling. It is divided in [various schools and styles](/wiki/Comparison_of_karate_styles "Comparison of karate styles"), which distinguishes techniques, training methods, among other things. Some styles, especially [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin") and other [full contact](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate") styles, has proven to be effective in MMA as it is one of the core foundations of kickboxing, and specializes in striking techniques.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-karate_UFC-135)[[135]](#cite_note-136) Karate from all styles has also been a common base, with many getting introduced to martial arts and combat sports by training Karate in their youth. Various styles of karate are practiced by some MMA fighters, notably Stephen [Chuck Liddell](/wiki/Chuck_Liddell "Chuck Liddell"), [Bas Rutten](/wiki/Bas_Rutten "Bas Rutten"), [Lyoto Machida](/wiki/Lyoto_Machida "Lyoto Machida"), [Stephen Thompson](/wiki/Stephen_Thompson_(fighter) "Stephen Thompson (fighter)"), [John Makdessi](/wiki/John_Makdessi "John Makdessi"), [Uriah Hall](/wiki/Uriah_Hall "Uriah Hall"), [Erik Pérez](/wiki/Erik_P%C3%A9rez "Erik Pérez"), [Ryan Jimmo](/wiki/Ryan_Jimmo "Ryan Jimmo"), [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre"), [Kyoji Horiguchi](/wiki/Kyoji_Horiguchi "Kyoji Horiguchi"), [Giga Chikadze](/wiki/Giga_Chikadze "Giga Chikadze"), [Robert Whittaker](/wiki/Robert_Whittaker_(fighter) "Robert Whittaker (fighter)"), [Henry Cejudo](/wiki/Henry_Cejudo "Henry Cejudo"), and [Louis Gaudinot](/wiki/Louis_Gaudinot "Louis Gaudinot"). Liddell is known to have an extensive striking background in [Kenpō with Fabio Martella](/wiki/American_Kenpo "American Kenpo").[[136]](#cite_note-137) Lyoto Machida practices Shotokan Ryu,[[137]](#cite_note-138) and St-Pierre practices [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin").[[138]](#cite_note-knucklepit-139)

### Wushu Sanda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Wushu Sanda")]

Main article: [Sanshou](/wiki/Sanshou "Sanshou")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing wushu](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_wushu "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing wushu")

Sanda, or Sanshou, is one of the two disciplines of [sport *wushu*](/wiki/Sport_wushu "Sport wushu"). It is a modernized and full contact version of *wushu*, created in the late 20th century as a condensation of traditional Chinese [kung fu](/wiki/Kung_fu "Kung fu") techniques to be used in a [full contact](/wiki/Full-contact "Full-contact") competition environment.[[139]](#cite_note-Dynasty_Clothing-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141) It is a kickboxing style which has [punching](/wiki/Punch_(combat) "Punch (combat)"), [kicking](/wiki/Kicking "Kicking"), some use of elbows and [knee strikes](/wiki/Knee_strikes "Knee strikes")—similar to Kickboxing or Muay Thai— but it has the distinction of allowing a range of [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)"), [throws](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)") and [sweeps](/wiki/Sweep_(martial_arts) "Sweep (martial arts)"), similar to judo and wrestling.[[139]](#cite_note-Dynasty_Clothing-140)[[141]](#cite_note-Payne-2017-142)

They can be highly effective in competition due to their mixture of striking and takedowns, which can be easily synthesized with the rest of MMA training, such as groundfighting.[[141]](#cite_note-Payne-2017-142) It is prominently used by fighters from China, but it has found a following amongst many fighters around the world.[[141]](#cite_note-Payne-2017-142) Chief amongst these fighters is [Cung Le](/wiki/Cung_Le "Cung Le"), who is most notable for his TKO and KO victories over former UFC champions Frank Shamrock and Rich Franklin, and UFC strawweight champion [Zhang Weili](/wiki/Zhang_Weili "Zhang Weili"), the first Chinese champion in the UFC. Other wushu sanshou based fighters who have entered MMA include [Michael Page](/wiki/Michael_Page "Michael Page"), [Song Yadong](/wiki/Song_Yadong "Song Yadong"), [K. J. Noons](/wiki/K._J._Noons "K. J. Noons"), [Pat Barry](/wiki/Pat_Barry_(fighter) "Pat Barry (fighter)"), [Zhang Tiequan](/wiki/Zhang_Tiequan "Zhang Tiequan"),[[142]](#cite_note-143) [Muslim Salihov](/wiki/Muslim_Salihov "Muslim Salihov"),[[143]](#cite_note-144) and [Zabit Magomedsharipov](/wiki/Zabit_Magomedsharipov "Zabit Magomedsharipov").[[144]](#cite_note-145)

### Taekwondo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Taekwondo")]

Main article: [Taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing taekwondo](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_taekwondo "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing taekwondo")

Taekwondo is a [Korean martial art](/wiki/Korean_martial_art "Korean martial art"), emerging in the 1950s as a mixture between Japanese Karate, traditional Korean martial arts and some Chinese kung fu. It is a striking-based style with heavy focus on various styles of kicking, such as head-height kicks, spinning jump kicks, and fast kicking techniques.[[145]](#cite_note-146) Several accomplished MMA fighters have an extensive background in taekwondo, and many were introduced to martial arts through it.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148) Some fighters who use taekwondo techniques in MMA are former UFC lightweight champion and WEC lightweight champion [Anthony Pettis](/wiki/Anthony_Pettis "Anthony Pettis"), who is 3rd dan black belt as well as an instructor,[[148]](#cite_note-fightmagazine.com-149) [Benson Henderson](/wiki/Benson_Henderson "Benson Henderson"), [Yair Rodriguez](/wiki/Yair_Rodriguez "Yair Rodriguez"), [Marco Ruas](/wiki/Marco_Ruas "Marco Ruas") and former UFC middleweight champion [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva"), who is a 5th dan black belt.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

In his instructional book, Anderson Silva admitted the influence of taekwondo in the formation of his unique style. "In each of my fights, I tried to utilize techniques from all the various styles I had studied. I threw taekwondo kicks. I threw Muay Thai knees and elbows, and I used my knowledge of Brazilian jiu-jitsu on the ground."[[150]](#cite_note-SilvaKrauss2008-151) Anthony Pettis has also stated that he is "definitely a traditional martial artist first and a mixed martial artist second",[[148]](#cite_note-fightmagazine.com-149) as well as his "style of attacking is different [because of his] taekwondo background".[[151]](#cite_note-152)

Other notable fighters who have a base in Taekwondo or are known for using their Taekwondo skills while fighting include [Edson Barboza](/wiki/Edson_Barboza "Edson Barboza"), [Valentina Shevchenko](/wiki/Valentina_Shevchenko "Valentina Shevchenko"), [Benson Henderson](/wiki/Benson_Henderson "Benson Henderson"), [Yair Rodriguez](/wiki/Yair_Rodriguez "Yair Rodriguez"), [Anthony Pettis](/wiki/Anthony_Pettis "Anthony Pettis"), [Mirko Cro Cop](/wiki/Mirko_Cro_Cop "Mirko Cro Cop"), [Cory Sandhagen](/wiki/Cory_Sandhagen "Cory Sandhagen"), [Israel Adesanya](/wiki/Israel_Adesanya "Israel Adesanya") and [Conor McGregor](/wiki/Conor_McGregor "Conor McGregor").

### Capoeira

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Capoeira")]

Main article: [Capoeira](/wiki/Capoeira "Capoeira")

See also: [Mixed martial artists utilizing capoeira](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_utilizing_capoeira "Category:Mixed martial artists utilizing capoeira")

Capoeira is an [Afro-Brazilian](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") art form that incorporates elements of martial arts, games, music, and dance. Capoeira is often practiced as a form of dancing and game, but its origins lie as a concealed style of self-defense and combat, and can be used as such. It uses a style of fighting with quick and complex maneuvers, which use power, speed, and leverage across a wide variety of kicks, spins and techniques. Pure Capoeira is difficult to use in MMA due its complexity, but many fighters incorporated individual techniques into their reportoire.[[152]](#cite_note-153) Additionally, Capoeira has an importance to MMA history, as many capoeiristas participated in [Vale Tudo](/wiki/Vale_Tudo "Vale Tudo") challenges in Brazil against practitioners of other martial arts, in particular with a rivalry with Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu.[[153]](#cite_note-154) In 1995 at the *Desafio Internacional de Vale Tudo* event, Capoeirista Mestre Hulk defeated two-time BJJ world champion [Amaury Bitetti](/wiki/Amaury_Bitetti "Amaury Bitetti") using Capoeira techniques in an underdog victory.[[154]](#cite_note-155)

Some fighters which have trained Capoeira and incorporated techniques include [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva"), [Conor McGregor](/wiki/Conor_McGregor "Conor McGregor"), [Deiveson Figueiredo](/wiki/Deiveson_Figueiredo "Deiveson Figueiredo"), [Thiago Santos](/wiki/Thiago_Santos "Thiago Santos"), [Marco Ruas](/wiki/Marco_Ruas "Marco Ruas") and [Michel Pereira](/wiki/Michel_Pereira "Michel Pereira").

### Savate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Savate")]

Main article: [Savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate")

Although not as common as other disciplines, some fighters have used Savate effectively in MMA. Savate restricts the use of shins and knees, allowing only foot kicks. It focuses on kicking more than punching, and its kicks are characteristically very fast, mobile and flexible. It also possesses a complex and evasive footwork. However, because Savatuers train wearing shoes, adjustments have to be made to how they throw kicks in MMA. Notable Savate fighters include [Karl Amoussou](/wiki/Karl_Amoussou "Karl Amoussou"), [Gerard Gordeau](/wiki/Gerard_Gordeau "Gerard Gordeau"), [Cheick Kongo](/wiki/Cheick_Kongo "Cheick Kongo"), and former Bellator Light Heavyweight Champion [Christian M'Pumbu](/wiki/Christian_M%27Pumbu "Christian M'Pumbu").

## Basic strategies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Basic strategies")]

Main page: [Mixed martial artists by strategy](/wiki/Category:Mixed_martial_artists_by_strategy "Category:Mixed martial artists by strategy")

The techniques utilized in mixed martial arts competition generally fall into two categories: [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") techniques (such as [kicks](/wiki/Kick "Kick"), [knees](/wiki/Knee_(strike) "Knee (strike)"), [punches](/wiki/Punch_(strike) "Punch (strike)") and [elbows](/wiki/Elbow_(strike) "Elbow (strike)")) and [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling") techniques (such as [clinch holds](/wiki/Clinch_hold "Clinch hold"), [pinning holds](/wiki/Pinning_hold "Pinning hold"), [submission holds](/wiki/Submission_hold "Submission hold"), [sweeps](/wiki/Sweep_(grappling) "Sweep (grappling)"), [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") and [throws](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)")).

Today, mixed martial artists must [cross-train](/wiki/Cross-training "Cross-training") in a variety of styles to counter their opponent's strengths and remain effective in all the phases of combat.

### Sprawl-and-Brawl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Sprawl-and-Brawl")]

Main page: [Sprawl-and-brawl fighters](/wiki/Category:Sprawl-and-brawl_fighters "Category:Sprawl-and-brawl fighters")

Sprawl-and-Brawl is a [stand-up fighting](/wiki/Stand-up_fighting "Stand-up fighting") tactic that consists of effective stand-up striking while avoiding [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting") typically by using [sprawls](/wiki/Sprawl_(grappling) "Sprawl (grappling)") to defend against [takedowns](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") or [throws](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)").[[114]](#cite_note-Kendall_Shields-2010-115)

A Sprawl-and-Brawler is usually a [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [kickboxer](/wiki/Kickbox "Kickbox"), or [karateka](/wiki/Karate "Karate") who has trained in various styles of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), and/or [sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)") to avoid takedowns to keep the fight standing. This is a form which is heavily practiced in the amateur leagues.

These fighters will often study [submission grappling](/wiki/Submission_grappling "Submission grappling") to avoid being forced into submission should they find themselves on the ground. This style can be deceptively different from traditional kickboxing styles, since sprawl-and-brawlers must adapt their techniques to incorporate [takedown](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") and [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting") defense. A few notable examples are [Igor Vovchanchyn](/wiki/Igor_Vovchanchyn "Igor Vovchanchyn"), [Mirko Filipović](/wiki/Mirko_Filipovi%C4%87 "Mirko Filipović"), [Chuck Liddell](/wiki/Chuck_Liddell "Chuck Liddell"), [Mark Hunt](/wiki/Mark_Hunt "Mark Hunt") and more recently [Alex Pereira](/wiki/Alex_Pereira "Alex Pereira"), [Francis Ngannou](/wiki/Francis_Ngannou "Francis Ngannou"), [Junior dos Santos](/wiki/Junior_dos_Santos "Junior dos Santos"), [Justin Gaethje](/wiki/Justin_Gaethje "Justin Gaethje"), [Andrei Arlovski](/wiki/Andrei_Arlovski "Andrei Arlovski"),[[155]](#cite_note-156) and [Joanna Jędrzejczyk](/wiki/Joanna_J%C4%99drzejczyk "Joanna Jędrzejczyk").[[156]](#cite_note-157)

### Ground-and-pound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Ground-and-pound")]

Main page: [Ground-and-pound fighters](/wiki/Category:Ground-and-pound_fighters "Category:Ground-and-pound fighters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Gina_Carano_Ground-and-Pound.png/250px-Gina_Carano_Ground-and-Pound.png)](/wiki/File:Gina_Carano_Ground-and-Pound.png)

[Gina Carano](/wiki/Gina_Carano "Gina Carano") applying a ground-and-pound on her opponent

Ground-and-pound is a strategy consisting of taking an opponent to the ground using a [takedown](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)") or throw, obtaining a top, or [dominant grappling position](/wiki/Grappling_position "Grappling position"), and then striking the opponent repeatedly, primarily with fists, [hammerfists](/wiki/Strike_(attack)#Hammerfist "Strike (attack)"), and elbows. Ground-and-pound is also used as a precursor to attempting [submission](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sport_term) "Submission (combat sport term)") holds.

The style is used by fighters well-versed in submission defense and skilled at takedowns. They take the fight to the ground, maintain a grappling position, and strike until their opponent submits or is [knocked out](/wiki/Knocked_out "Knocked out"). Although not a traditional style of striking, the effectiveness and reliability of ground-and-pound has made it a popular tactic. It was first demonstrated as an effective technique by [Mark Coleman](/wiki/Mark_Coleman "Mark Coleman"), then popularized by fighters such as [Chael Sonnen](/wiki/Chael_Sonnen "Chael Sonnen"), [Glover Teixeira](/wiki/Glover_Teixeira "Glover Teixeira"), [Don Frye](/wiki/Don_Frye "Don Frye"), [Frank Trigg](/wiki/Frank_Trigg "Frank Trigg"), [Jon Jones](/wiki/Jon_Jones "Jon Jones"), [Cheick Kongo](/wiki/Cheick_Kongo "Cheick Kongo"), [Mark Kerr](/wiki/Mark_Kerr_(fighter) "Mark Kerr (fighter)"), [Frank Shamrock](/wiki/Frank_Shamrock "Frank Shamrock"), [Tito Ortiz](/wiki/Tito_Ortiz "Tito Ortiz"), [Matt Hughes](/wiki/Matt_Hughes_(fighter) "Matt Hughes (fighter)"), [Daniel Cormier](/wiki/Daniel_Cormier "Daniel Cormier"), [Chris Weidman](/wiki/Chris_Weidman "Chris Weidman"), and [Khabib Nurmagomedov](/wiki/Khabib_Nurmagomedov "Khabib Nurmagomedov").[[113]](#cite_note-Yahoo-2009-114)

While most fighters use ground-and-pound statically, by way of holding their opponents down and hitting them with short strikes from the top position, a few fighters manage to utilize it dynamically by striking their opponents while changing positions, thus not allowing their opponents to settle once they take them down. [Cain Velasquez](/wiki/Cain_Velasquez "Cain Velasquez") is one of the most devastating ground strikers in MMA and is known for continuing to strike his opponents on the ground while transitioning between positions.[[157]](#cite_note-158) [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko"), considered among the greatest masters of ground-and-pound in MMA history, was the first to demonstrate this dynamic style of striking in transition. He was striking his opponents on the ground while passing [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)"), or while his opponents were attempting to recover guard.[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)

In the year 2000, MMA play-by-play commentator [Stephen Quadros](/wiki/Stephen_Quadros "Stephen Quadros") coined the popular phrase *lay and pray*. This refers to a situation where a wrestler or grappler keeps another fighter pinned or controlled on the mat, throwing light strikes to avoid a stand up, yet exhibit little urgency to finish the grounded opponent with a knockout or a submission for the majority or entirety of the fight, looking for a decision win through high control time.[[160]](#cite_note-bleacherreport.com-161) The implication of "lay and pray" is that after the wrestler/grappler takes the striker down and 'lays' on them to neutralize the opponent's striking weapons, they 'pray' that the referee does not return them to the standing position. This style is considered by many fans as the most boring style of fighting and is highly criticized for intentionally creating non-action, yet it is effective. Some argue that 'lay-and-pray' is justified and that it is the responsibility of the downed fighter to be able to protect themselves from this legitimate technique.[[160]](#cite_note-bleacherreport.com-161)[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164) Former [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") Welterweight champion [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre") has been criticized by fans for playing it safe and applying the lay-and-pray tactic in his fights,[[164]](#cite_note-165) as has former [Bellator MMA](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA") Welterweight champion [Ben Askren](/wiki/Ben_Askren "Ben Askren"), who justified the tactic, explaining that championship fights are much harder, as they are five rounds long compared with the usual three.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Carnage_at_the_Creek_6_-_triangle_armbar.jpg/250px-Carnage_at_the_Creek_6_-_triangle_armbar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnage_at_the_Creek_6_-_triangle_armbar.jpg)

MMA fighter attempts a [Triangle](/wiki/Triangle_choke "Triangle choke")-[Armbar](/wiki/Armbar "Armbar") submission on his opponent.

### Submission-seeking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Submission-seeking")]

Submission-seeking is a reference to the strategy of taking an opponent to the ground using a takedown or [throw](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)") and then applying a [submission hold](/wiki/Submission_hold "Submission hold"), forcing the opponent to [submit](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sport_term) "Submission (combat sport term)"). While grapplers will often work to attain dominant position, some may be more comfortable fighting from other positions (ex. fighters pulling [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)")). It enable fighters to force opponents into submission through joint locks or chokes. Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ) plays a significant role in MMA submission grappling, with techniques such as armbars, triangles, rear-naked chokes, guillotines, and kimuras being commonly utilized.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

If a grappler finds themselves unable to force a takedown, they may resort to *pulling [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)")*, whereby they voluntarily pull their opponent into a dominant position on the ground.[[167]](#cite_note-168) This was one of the first fighting styles that had shown success, popularized by BJJ exponent [Royce Gracie](/wiki/Royce_Gracie "Royce Gracie") during early UFC events.
Submissions are an essential part of many disciplines, most notably [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu"), [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling"), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)"), [luta livre](/wiki/Luta_livre "Luta livre") and [shoot wrestling](/wiki/Shoot_wrestling "Shoot wrestling"). Submission-based styles were popularized in the early UFC events by [Royce Gracie](/wiki/Royce_Gracie "Royce Gracie") and [Ken Shamrock](/wiki/Ken_Shamrock "Ken Shamrock"), and were the dominant tactic in the early UFCs. Modern proponents of the submission-seeking style, such as [Demian Maia](/wiki/Demian_Maia "Demian Maia"), [Ronaldo Souza](/wiki/Ronaldo_Souza "Ronaldo Souza"), [Charles Oliveira](/wiki/Charles_Oliveira "Charles Oliveira"), [Ryan Hall](/wiki/Ryan_Hall_(grappler) "Ryan Hall (grappler)"), [Marcin Held](/wiki/Marcin_Held "Marcin Held"), and [Paul Craig](/wiki/Paul_Craig "Paul Craig") tend to come from a [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") background.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

### Clinch-fighting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Clinch-fighting")]

Clinch-Fighting is a tactic consisting of using a [clinch hold](/wiki/Clinch_hold "Clinch hold") to prevent the opponent from moving away into more distant striking range, while also attempting takedowns or throws and [striking](/wiki/Strike_(attack) "Strike (attack)") the opponent using [knees](/wiki/Knee_(strike) "Knee (strike)"), [stomps](/wiki/Stomp_(strike) "Stomp (strike)"), [elbows](/wiki/Elbow_(strike) "Elbow (strike)"), and [punches](/wiki/Punch_(combat) "Punch (combat)"). The clinch is often utilized by wrestlers and judokas that have added components of the striking game (typically boxing), and [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") fighters.

[Ken Shamrock](/wiki/Ken_Shamrock "Ken Shamrock") was known for his impressive clinch work when he submitted [Dan Severn](/wiki/Dan_Severn "Dan Severn") with a standing guillotine choke at [UFC 6](/wiki/UFC_6 "UFC 6").

Wrestlers and judoka may use clinch fighting as a way to neutralize the superior striking skills of a stand-up fighter to prevent takedowns or throws by a superior ground fighter. [Ronda Rousey](/wiki/Ronda_Rousey "Ronda Rousey"), with her [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") background, is considered a master at initiating throws from the clinch to set up [armbars](/wiki/Armlock "Armlock").[[169]](#cite_note-Marrocco-170)

The [clinch](/wiki/Thai-clinch "Thai-clinch") or "plum" of a [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai") fighter is often used to improve the accuracy of [knees](/wiki/Knee_(strike) "Knee (strike)") and [elbows](/wiki/Elbow_(strike) "Elbow (strike)") by physically controlling the position of the opponent. [Anderson Silva](/wiki/Anderson_Silva "Anderson Silva") is well known for his devastating Muay Thai clinch. He defeated UFC middle weight champion [Rich Franklin](/wiki/Rich_Franklin "Rich Franklin") using the Muay Thai clinch and kneeing Franklin repeatedly to the body and face—breaking Franklin's nose. In their rematch Silva repeated this and won again.[[170]](#cite_note-Slack-171)

Other fighters may use the clinch to push their opponent against the cage or ropes, where they can effectively control their opponent's movement and restrict mobility while striking them with punches to the body or stomps also known as *dirty boxing* or "Wall and Maul". [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture") used his [Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling") background to popularize this style en route to six title reigns in the UFC.[[171]](#cite_note-Sllack-172)

### Score-oriented fighting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Score-oriented fighting")]

Score-oriented fighting is a style that is based around trying to win through outscoring their opponent and winning a decision. Usually fighters who adopt this strategy use takedowns only for [scoring](/wiki/Score_(sport) "Score (sport)"), allowing the adversary to stand up and continue the fight. They also want to land clear strikes and control the octagon. In order to win the fight by decision, all score oriented fighters have to have strong defensive techniques and avoid takedowns.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

In general, fighters who cannot win fights through lightning offense, or are more suited to win fights in the later rounds or via decision are commonly known as *grinders*. Grinders aim to shut down their opponent's game plan and chip away at them via light strikes, clinching, smothering and ground-and-pound for most of the rounds. Prominent examples of grinders are [Sean Strickland](/wiki/Sean_Strickland "Sean Strickland"), who has a very defensive and stand-up striking focused style, and [Merab Dvalishvili](/wiki/Merab_Dvalishvili "Merab Dvalishvili") who has a very aggressive, wrestling focused style.

## Women's mixed martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Women's mixed martial arts")]

Main article: [Women's mixed martial arts](/wiki/Women%27s_mixed_martial_arts "Women's mixed martial arts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Megumi_Fujii_and_Cody_Welchlin_square_off_in_an_MMA_fight_%28DW%29.jpg/250px-Megumi_Fujii_and_Cody_Welchlin_square_off_in_an_MMA_fight_%28DW%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Megumi_Fujii_and_Cody_Welchlin_square_off_in_an_MMA_fight_(DW).jpg)

[Megumi Fujii](/wiki/Megumi_Fujii "Megumi Fujii") (left) facing [Cody Welchlin](/w/index.php?title=Cody_Welchlin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cody Welchlin (page does not exist)") (right)

While mixed martial arts is primarily a male dominated sport, it does have female athletes. Female competition in Japan includes promotions such as the all-female [Valkyrie](/wiki/Valkyrie_(mixed_martial_arts) "Valkyrie (mixed martial arts)"), and [Jewels](/wiki/Jewels_(mixed_martial_arts) "Jewels (mixed martial arts)") (formerly known as [Smackgirl](/wiki/Smackgirl "Smackgirl")).[[173]](#cite_note-174) However historically, there has been only a select few major professional mixed martial arts organizations in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") that invite women to compete. Among those are the [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC"), [Strikeforce](/wiki/Strikeforce_(mixed_martial_arts) "Strikeforce (mixed martial arts)"), [Bellator Fighting Championships](/wiki/Bellator_Fighting_Championships "Bellator Fighting Championships"), the all female [Invicta Fighting Championships](/wiki/Invicta_Fighting_Championships "Invicta Fighting Championships"), and the now defunct [EliteXC](/wiki/EliteXC "EliteXC").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There has been a growing awareness of women in mixed martial arts due to popular female fighters and personalities such as [Ronda Rousey](/wiki/Ronda_Rousey "Ronda Rousey"), [Megumi Fujii](/wiki/Megumi_Fujii "Megumi Fujii"), [Miesha Tate](/wiki/Miesha_Tate "Miesha Tate"), [Cristiane Santos](/wiki/Cristiane_Santos "Cristiane Santos"), [Joanna Jędrzejczyk](/wiki/Joanna_J%C4%99drzejczyk "Joanna Jędrzejczyk"), [Holly Holm](/wiki/Holly_Holm "Holly Holm"), [Alexa Grasso](/wiki/Alexa_Grasso "Alexa Grasso") and [Gina Carano](/wiki/Gina_Carano "Gina Carano") among others.

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: History")]

In Japan, female competition has been documented since the mid-1990s. Influenced by female professional wrestling and [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing"), the Smackgirl competition was formed in 2001 and became the only major all-female promotion in mixed martial arts. Other early successful Japanese female organizations included [Ladies Legend Pro-Wrestling](/wiki/Ladies_Legend_Pro-Wrestling "Ladies Legend Pro-Wrestling"), ReMix (a predecessor to Smackgirl), U-Top Tournament, K-Grace, and AX.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Aside from all-female organizations, most major Japanese male dominated promotions have held select female competitions. These have included [DEEP](/wiki/Deep_(mixed_martial_arts) "Deep (mixed martial arts)"), MARS, Gladiator, HEAT, Cage Force, [K-1](/wiki/K-1 "K-1"), [Sengoku](/wiki/World_Victory_Road "World Victory Road"), [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") (under the name G-Shooto), and [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase") (under the name Pancrase Athena).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the United States, prior to the success of *[The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter")* reality show that launched mixed martial arts into the mainstream media,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] there was no major coverage of female competitions. Some early organizations who invited women to compete included, International Fighting Championships, SuperBrawl, [King of the Cage](/wiki/King_of_the_Cage "King of the Cage"), Rage in the Cage, Ring of Combat, Bas Rutten Invitational, and HOOKnSHOOT. From the mid-2000s, more coverage came when organizations such as [Strikeforce](/wiki/Strikeforce_(mixed_martial_arts) "Strikeforce (mixed martial arts)"), [EliteXC](/wiki/EliteXC "EliteXC"), [Bellator Fighting Championships](/wiki/Bellator_Fighting_Championships "Bellator Fighting Championships"), and [Shark Fights](/wiki/Shark_Fights "Shark Fights") invited women to compete.

Outside Japan and the United States, female competition is almost exclusively found in minor local promotions. However, in Europe some major organizations have held select female competitions, including [It's Showtime](/wiki/It%27s_Showtime_(kickboxing) "It's Showtime (kickboxing)"), Shooto Europe, [Cage Warriors](/wiki/Cage_Warriors "Cage Warriors"), and [M-1 Global](/wiki/M-1_Global "M-1 Global").

Following Zuffa's acquisition of Strikeforce in March 2011,[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-178) the UFC began promoting women's fights, with Ronda Rousey rapidly becoming one of the promotion's biggest draws.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

Controversy arose in 2013, when CFA (Championship Fighting Alliance) fighter [Fallon Fox](/wiki/Fallon_Fox "Fallon Fox") came out as a [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") woman. The case became a centerpiece of debates concerning whether it was fair to have a transgender woman compete against [cisgender](/wiki/Cisgender "Cisgender") women in a contact sport.[[179]](#cite_note-180) Neither the UFC nor Invicta FC says they will allow her to fight, and then-UFC Bantamweight champion Ronda Rousey said she would not fight her.[[180]](#cite_note-181)

## Amateur mixed martial arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Amateur mixed martial arts")]

Amateur Mixed Martial Arts is the [amateur version](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports") of the Mixed Martial Arts in which participants engage largely or entirely without remuneration. Under the [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation") (IMMAF) and World MMA Association (WMMAA), it is practiced within a safe and regulated environment which relies on a fair and objective scoring system and competition procedures similar to those in force in the [professional Mixed Martial Arts rules](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_rules "Mixed martial arts rules").[[181]](#cite_note-182)[[182]](#cite_note-183)
Amateur MMA is practiced with board shorts and with approved protection gear that includes shin protectors, and amateur [MMA gloves](/wiki/MMA_gloves "MMA gloves").

The International Mixed Martial Arts Federation and the World Mixed Martial Arts Association announced an amalgamation on April 11, 2018, uniting the two organizations behind one bid for Olympic sport recognition after being instructed by Global Association of International Sport Federations (GAISF). The WMMAA and the IMMAF signed a legally binding affiliation [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") (MOU) in May 2018 and finalized the agreement by November 2018, along with the first unified world championships.[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185)

The Global Association of Mixed Martial Arts (GAMMA) was founded in Rome through Paolo Biotti's initiative on July 17, 2018.[[185]](#cite_note-Levy-2019-186)[[186]](#cite_note-Taylor-2019-187) From 8 to 12 March 2024, mixed martial arts was included as a demonstration sport in the 2023 African Games in Accra, Ghana, under GAMMA.[[187]](#cite_note-insidethegames.biz-2024-188)[[188]](#cite_note-msn.com-189)[[189]](#cite_note-Redaction-2024-190)[[190]](#cite_note-Akakpo-2024-191) From 11 to 13 July 2024, GAMMA member federations participated in the 2nd Asian Mixed Martial Arts Championships organised by the Asian Mixed Martial Arts Association (AMMA) under the Olympic Council of Asia.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

### World Mixed Martial Arts Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: World Mixed Martial Arts Association")]

World Mixed Martial Arts Association (WMMAA) was founded in 2012 in Monaco by [M-1 Global](/wiki/M-1_Global "M-1 Global") commercial promoters and is under the leadership of the President Finkelstein and [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko").[[192]](#cite_note-193)[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196) The World MMA Association was an organization that managed and developed mixed martial arts, establishing rules and procedures, hosting MMA competitions.

On October 20, 2013, the first World MMA Championship was held in Saint Petersburg, Russia.[[196]](#cite_note-197)

By December 2013, WMMAA had 38 member states representing the sport and registered in accordance with national laws. By 2017, WMMAA had expanded to 83 members: Afghanistan, Albania, Armenia, Austria, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Belgium, Bulgaria, Canada, Croatia, Colombia, Czech Republic, France, Guatemala, Georgia, Greece, India, Iran, Italy, Kazakhstan, Latvia, Lithuania, Macedonia, Moldova, Monaco, Mongolia, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Russia, Senegal, Serbia, Slovakia, South Korea, Spain, Switzerland, Tajikistan, Trinidad and Tobago, Turkey, Ukraine, Uzbekistan, and Venezuela.[[197]](#cite_note-198)

### International Mixed Martial Arts Federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: International Mixed Martial Arts Federation")]

Main article: [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation")

On February 29, 2012, the International Mixed Martial Arts Federation (IMMAF) was set up to bring international structure, development and support to mixed martial arts worldwide.[[198]](#cite_note-199) IMMAF launched with support of market leader, the [Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC)](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship_(UFC) "Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC)").[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201) The IMMAF is a non-profit, democratic federation organized according to international federation standards to ensure that MMA as a sport is allowed the same recognition, representation and rights as all other major sports. The IMMAF is registered under Swedish law and is founded on democratic principles, as outlined in their statutes.[[201]](#cite_note-202) As of 2025, there were 128 total members.[[202]](#cite_note-203)

The IMMAF held its first Amateur World Championships in Las Vegas, US, from June 30 to July 6, 2014.[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206)

### Global Association of Mixed Martial Arts (GAMMA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Global Association of Mixed Martial Arts (GAMMA)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/AFRICANGAMES_FINAL-80.jpg/250px-AFRICANGAMES_FINAL-80.jpg)](/wiki/File:AFRICANGAMES_FINAL-80.jpg)

GAMMA medallists on the podium at the 2023 African Games

The Global Association of Mixed Martial Arts (GAMMA) was founded in 2018 as the international governing body for mixed martial arts, with the support of main sponsor [ONE Championship](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship").[[185]](#cite_note-Levy-2019-186)[[186]](#cite_note-Taylor-2019-187) It aims are to achieve official international sport recognition for mixed martial arts, to develop the sport and its governance structures globally and promote its growth and benefits in communities worldwide. Its vision is Olympic Games inclusion.[[206]](#cite_note-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208)[[187]](#cite_note-insidethegames.biz-2024-188) GAMMA is a non-profit Federation headquartered in Lausanne (Switzerland), and chaired by Tommy Paulus Hermawan.[[208]](#cite_note-Executive_Board-209) GAMMA has 80 national federations as members across 5 continents[[208]](#cite_note-Executive_Board-209) and organises international, nations tournaments around the world under amateur MMA rules.[[209]](#cite_note-210)[[210]](#cite_note-211)[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213) In 2024, GAMMA achieved the inclusion of mixed martial arts as a demonstration discipline in the 2023 African Games in Accra.[[187]](#cite_note-insidethegames.biz-2024-188)[[188]](#cite_note-msn.com-189)[[189]](#cite_note-Redaction-2024-190)[[190]](#cite_note-Akakpo-2024-191)

## Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Strikeforce_trainer_2010-01-07.jpg/250px-Strikeforce_trainer_2010-01-07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strikeforce_trainer_2010-01-07.jpg)

A ring-side doctor attends to a fighter following a loss.

Mixed Martial Arts competitions have changed dramatically since the first Ultimate Fighting
Championship in 1993, specifically with the inception of the [Unified Rules of Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts_rules#Unified_rules "Mixed martial arts rules"). There remains a paucity of data on injuries that occur in MMA, and resulting concerns with regard to MMA's safety remain. A 2014 systematic review concluded that the injury incidence rate in MMA appears to be greater than in most, if not all, other popular and commonly practiced combat sports.[[213]](#cite_note-Lystad,_R.P._2014-214)

### Injury rates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Injury rates")]

In a 2014 meta-analysis of the available injury data in MMA, the injury incidence rate was estimated to be 228.7 injuries per 1000 athlete-exposures (one athlete-exposure is defined as one athlete participating in a single fight).[[213]](#cite_note-Lystad,_R.P._2014-214) The estimated injury incidence rate in MMA is greater than in other full-contact combat sports such as [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") (44.0 injuries per 1000 athlete-exposures),[[214]](#cite_note-Frey,_A._2004-215) [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo") (79.4 injuries per 1000 athlete-exposures),[[215]](#cite_note-Lystad,_R.P._2009-216) [amateur boxing](/wiki/Amateur_boxing "Amateur boxing") (77.7 injuries per 1000 athlete-exposures),[[216]](#cite_note-Estwanik,_J.J._1984-217) and [professional boxing](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing") (118.0–250.6 injuries per 1000 athlete-exposures).[[217]](#cite_note-Bledsloe,_G.H._2005-218)[[218]](#cite_note-Zazryn,_T.R._2003-219)[[219]](#cite_note-Zazryn,_T.R._2006-220)[[220]](#cite_note-Zazryn,_T.R._2009-221)

### Injury pattern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Injury pattern")]

In general, the injury pattern in MMA is very similar to that in professional boxing but unlike that found in other combat sports such as judo and taekwondo.[[213]](#cite_note-Lystad,_R.P._2014-214) The most commonly injured body region is the head (66.8% to 78.0% of reported injuries) followed by the wrist/hand (6.0% to 12.0% of reported injuries), while the most frequent types of injury were laceration (36.7% to 59.4% of reported injuries), fracture (7.4% to 43.3% of reported injuries), and concussion (3.8% to 20.4% of reported injuries).[[213]](#cite_note-Lystad,_R.P._2014-214) The frequency of impact to the ear and low utilization of ear protection leads to a high frequency of perichondral hematoma that can lead to [cauliflower ear](/wiki/Cauliflower_ear "Cauliflower ear").[[221]](#cite_note-222)

### Brain injury and CTE

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Brain injury and CTE")]

See also: [Chronic traumatic encephalopathy](/wiki/Chronic_traumatic_encephalopathy "Chronic traumatic encephalopathy")

The repeated head strikes received by MMA competitors causes frequent concussions and permanent [brain damage](/wiki/Brain_damage "Brain damage").[[222]](#cite_note-223) Also, the repeated head trauma in contact sports, especially combat sports, are directly related to a brain disease called [chronic traumatic encephalopathy](/wiki/Chronic_traumatic_encephalopathy "Chronic traumatic encephalopathy") (CTE). This disease begins to develop during the life of the athlete, and continues to develop even after sports activity has ceased.[[223]](#cite_note-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225)[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-227)[[227]](#cite_note-228)[[228]](#cite_note-229)

Because the disease is detected post-mortem, and MMA as a sport is relatively young, there are still few cases of CTE recognized in MMA, although there are more and more suspected and confirmed cases of CTE.[[229]](#cite_note-230)[[230]](#cite_note-231) In preliminary results reported in April 2012 as part of an ongoing study of a 109 professional boxers and MMA fighters being conducted by Charles Bernick and his colleagues at [Cleveland Clinic's Lou Ruvo Center for Brain Health](/wiki/Cleveland_Clinic_Lou_Ruvo_Center_for_Brain_Health "Cleveland Clinic Lou Ruvo Center for Brain Health"), fighters with more than six years of ring experience were observed to have reductions in size in their [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") and [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), while fighters with more than twelve years of ring experience were observed to have both reductions in size and symptoms such as [memory loss](/wiki/Memory_loss "Memory loss") (the hippocampus and thalamus deal with memory and alertness). Bernick speculates that the cumulative damage over time from lesser blows may eventually prove an even more important topic of study than that of infrequent concussions.[[231]](#cite_note-232)

In January 2021, veteran UFC fighter [Spencer Fisher](/wiki/Spencer_Fisher "Spencer Fisher") confirmed to MMA Fighting that he suffered from all the symptoms of CTE: "I'm just beat up, and it's just gotten worse, like all my injuries are catching up with me now, aside from the brain thing, which is the biggest one, because it adds, it adds the depression, and putting things, thoughts together and staying on track." Later, [Dana White](/wiki/Dana_White "Dana White") ruled on this: "He's not the first and he's definitely not going to be the last. This is a contact sport and anybody who's ever done this younger, myself included, is dealing with brain issues. It's part of the gig."[[232]](#cite_note-233) In late 2021, Rose Gracie, daughter of UFC founder [Rorion Gracie](/wiki/Rorion_Gracie "Rorion Gracie"), in an interview about CTE on the *Trocação Franca* podcast, called the lack of CTE awareness in MMA 'criminal negligence'.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

### Fatalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Fatalities")]

Main article: [Fatalities in mixed martial arts contests](/wiki/Fatalities_in_mixed_martial_arts_contests "Fatalities in mixed martial arts contests")

There have been seven known deaths in MMA to date.[[234]](#cite_note-martialhistory-235)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
There were no documented cases of deaths after a sanctioned MMA event prior to 2007.[[235]](#cite_note-Houston_Vasquez-236)

Since 2007, there have been six fatalities in mixed martial arts matches. The first was the death of [Sam Vasquez](/wiki/Sam_Vasquez "Sam Vasquez") on November 30, 2007.[[236]](#cite_note-Vasquez_death-237) Vasquez collapsed shortly after being knocked out by Vince Libardi in the third round of an October 20, 2007, fight at the [Toyota Center](/wiki/Toyota_Center "Toyota Center") in Houston, Texas.[[235]](#cite_note-Houston_Vasquez-236) Vasquez had two separate surgeries to remove blood clots from his brain, and shortly after the second operation suffered a stroke and did not regain consciousness.[[236]](#cite_note-Vasquez_death-237)

The second death stemming from a sanctioned mixed martial arts contest occurred in South Carolina on June 28, 2010, when 30-year-old Michael Kirkham was knocked out and never regained consciousness. He was pronounced dead two days after the fight.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

The third death on August 11, 2012, involved 30 year old Tyrone Mims, who was making his amateur MMA debut at "Conflict MMA: Fight Night at the Point VI" in South Carolina, making his the second MMA-related death in the state.[[238]](#cite_note-239) After being TKO'd in the second round of the fight he became unresponsive and was taken to Medical University Hospital, where he was pronounced dead one hour later. No evidence of brain trauma or concussion was found and the initial autopsy has proved inconclusive. Coroner Rae Wooten explained that his death may probably have been from an irregular heartbeat caused by over exertion; however whether or not his death was a direct result of his fight remains a mystery.[[239]](#cite_note-240)

On February 27, 2014, 29-year-old Booto Guylain was transported to Johannesburg General Hospital to be treated for swelling and bleeding on the brain after suffering a KO loss via elbow in the last round of his fight in South African promotion "Extreme Fighting Championship Africa". He was unable to make a recovery, and after one week in the hospital he was pronounced dead.[[240]](#cite_note-241)[[241]](#cite_note-242)

On April 9, 2016, 28-year-old [João Carvalho](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Carvalho_(fighter) "João Carvalho (fighter)"), died following a Total Extreme Fighting event at the National Stadium in Dublin (Ireland). The Portuguese fighter was beaten by technical knockout in a fight on 9 April 2016 against [Charlie Ward](/wiki/Charlie_Ward_(fighter) "Charlie Ward (fighter)"), and fell ill twenty minutes after the fight. He was taken immediately to Beaumont hospital, where he underwent emergency brain surgery, but died two days later.[[242]](#cite_note-243)

On July 15, 2017, after a TKO loss resulting from unanswered punches in the second round of his fight, 37 year old Donshay White became unresponsive and collapsed in his locker room and was rushed to the KentuckyOne Health Sts. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital in Kentucky.[[243]](#cite_note-244) He was soon pronounced dead and was revealed to be caused by hypertensive/atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

## Legality of professional competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Legality of professional competitions")]

Main article: [Legality of professional MMA competitions](/wiki/Legality_of_professional_MMA_competitions "Legality of professional MMA competitions")

Professional MMA competitions enjoy different levels of legality in different countries. This is dependent on the laws of the specific state.

The [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation") attempts to provide an internationally recognised set of standards which will be universally adhered to.[[245]](#cite_note-246) However, they have no basis in national law and are meant as advisement only. Whereby regulation exists, however, the likelihood of MMA being legally recognised as a legitimate sport is increased. This was the case with France who banned it as a sport in 2016, due to a lack of regulation, then unbanned it in 2020 after a French federation began regulating it.[[246]](#cite_note-247)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixed_martial_arts&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** Prior to the widespread adoption of "mixed martial arts", or "MMA", other names were used to refer to what is now known as "MMA". Early in the sport's history, it was typically referred to as "No Holds Barred", or "NHB", in the United States[[1]](#cite_note-1) and "[Vale Tudo](/wiki/Vale_Tudo "Vale Tudo")" in Brazil.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Two of the earliest MMA promotion companies in Japan, [Shooto](/wiki/Shooto "Shooto") and [Pancrase](/wiki/Pancrase "Pancrase"), referred to the sport as "Shooting" (Shooto)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-blood1-4) and "Hybrid Wrestling" (Pancrase),[[5]](#cite_note-5) respectively. The sport is also commonly referred to colloquially as "ultimate fighting",[[6]](#cite_note-6) "[shootfighting](/wiki/Shootfighting "Shootfighting")",[[7]](#cite_note-7) and "cage fighting";[[8]](#cite_note-8) it was often referred to derisively in the United States as "human cockfighting".[[9]](#cite_note-9)
